###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-17T20:24:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4G5ff.526332$tl2.290982@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Bq1ff.3315$i7.134@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <Bq1ff.3315$i7.134@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> Sorry, Jimmy, I give up. Donald is correct: I keep getting the run around on 
> price.

Sorry for following - I'm so bloody long-winded :-

Well I had hoped otherwise - but if you are getting the run around - 
that's indicative that they aren't playing it straight.

Inevitably this has to start with the bean-counters. There is that very 
up front concern now in today's commerce, 'Ensure you make profits, the 
shareholders expect an annual return'. Nothing new in that, and the 
reason stock company adventurers came into existence in the 1600s. 
Nevertheless, in our modern cut-throat commerce, 'Make a profit and give 
a dividend' is paramount to any CEO's thinking. His alternative - he 
gets golden-bowler-hatted, if he's smart and ensures he has a golden 
parachute clause.

So the bean-counters, based on projected units sold, and assuming they 
did their job correctly, will have come up with financial projections, 
using units sold and a mix of consumer-end prices - no big deal as a 
problem for any SpreadSheet package. So bean-counters plus management 
have arrived at a formula that gives them a 'safe' income by DEMANDING 
that you purchase those coupon books up front before you have even 
written a line of code. (Then of course throw in the License Keys, just 
to make it easier !)

Now it could well be that the Sales Force is 'tied into a mantra', or as 
promoters/marketers for the products, just aren't doing their jobs 
properly. They must feel inhibited by the rigid set of rules, and as 
marketers should be challenging the rigid rules for flexibility.

Can anybody think of a precedent for what is going on. 'Buy my product, 
but up front, I want a piece of the action, so here's a coupon book that 
you have to buy'. The only thing I can think of which comes close is the 
entertainment industry. Think, music (modern and even classical), movies 
on DVDs, book royalties. Certainly if radio/TV use music there are 
royalties - whether there is a package price paid up front, or from an 
audit the broadcasters pay after the event, I don't now. Movies, well we 
rent those through 'renters' so presumably they also might pay up front 
or even have to pay a royalty based on unit rentals. Now of course 
there's the added 'Pay as You View' - so the cable companies get into a 
royalty payment scheme as well. Books - used to be fairly straight 
forward, go to a retailer and buy a copy. Lovely idea - Google wanted to 
digitize books - but the book publishers have reacted unfavourably, 
although Microsoft apparently is trying to go ahead with a parallel scheme.

It's my guess that, per individual complaint, and most likely directed 
at management rather than the sales force, that they ARE bending the 
rules. Now as backup to the figures I was asking you for, only last 
night I wrote to four people in four countries, to see if they are 
prepared to give me dollar numbers applicable to their own situation.
Hopefully they will respond favourably - be interesting to see what 
numbers they come up with. 'No names, no pack-drill' - in anonymity we 
may be able to use those figures. So give it a few more days.

What if any figures did you get, even if they are incomplete, and which 
situation did they balk at - where were they reluctant to be specific.

Dammit ! If there is an injustice to us developers, let's get it 
resolved with compromise, for once and all. I said I had contacted four 
- I am now going to contact some more, additional countries on the 
Continent and I'll even stretch out to Asia.

It goes without saying that you just raising this topic here has already 
left a bad taste about Micro Focus with many. I absolutely LOVE the 
product - but if they continue without resolution, and somebody asks, 
'Where can I get a compiler ?' - you can well imagine the responses they 
are likely to get.

Their awareness, don't kid yourself it's only Stephen Gennard, or 
'Wiggy', (Simon Tobias) and Michael W. looking in and providing advice. 
(Michael W. ? I'm assuming he is California based - haven't a clue what 
he does. From the way he writes, I'm assuming he might be one of those 
people locked in a room to whom they throw the occasional bunch of 
bananas. Up to you Michael - exactly what is your role ;-) ). There are 
others looking in - I wouldn't mind betting Don Higgins, one of the CEO 
Tony Hill's lieutenants down in California.

So in terms of hoping to get anywhere with this - the following was at 
the back of my mind. Can't recall circumstances but once wrote to Don 
Higgins, having picked up his name from the Forum. As part of his reply 
he made the point that Tony Hill, and he himself as a 'converted' 
lieutenant, believed in an 'open door policy'. They picked this concept 
up from a paper written by somebody else - sorry don't have its 
reference - but it made for a good read.

I took them up on this a short while back and wrote directly to Tony 
Hill. My topic, absolutely nothing to do with Runtimes. It got tediously 
long because it was historical. I apologised in advance for it's length, 
quoting Churchill's preference for short/concise.

Didn't do any good in my case. Tony replied, "Interesting background. 
What do you want me to do ? Yes, and I like 'short'". "What do you want 
me to do ?", flummoxed me a bit. I was very slow on the uptake. I should 
have replied, "Pay me an annual stipend, and offer me a seat on your 
Board and THEN I will suggest what you could do". :-). But note he did 
reply and was courteous.

Regardless of what we find on figures from my other sources, if they 
oblige, I think we have a genuine beef. We have to test Tony's open door 
policy one more time, writing to him directly and see if we can get a 
compromise that leaves us developers in a comfort zone and they, M/F, 
are still making money, perhaps even more money.

(More money - I'm thinking of a situation where you produce a gizmo 
(Component) with a sale price of $50 marketed through the Internet, 
where conceivably you might sell 500 copies. But currently that is 
totally negated by their coupon-book approach. So at this point in time 
you just DON't produce that gizmo).

As to your subject title, "Is Micro Focus doomed ?". Not just yet, go to 
their site and check their financials for year ending April 2005. 
Long-term, like COBOL in general, they *might* be doomed. You can also 
pick up on a photo of Tony plus his background.

Keep your pecker up - let's see what figures I get back. Again, still 
would like to see yours, even if they are incomplete.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

*>-----------------------------------------------------------------------

GENTLE READER - Completely skip this if you want - just background.

Postcript : My reference to sales people - How things have changed.

A team of three - Geoff - initially ran incoming Inventory control back 
at Guildford and when we went down to Yeovil, Somerset, became Accounts 
Receivable Manager (approximately 200 clerks). Howerd - local boy in 
Yeovil, Manager Van Sales Accounting - inventory control of the 
individual van sales (warehouses on wheels). Yours truly ex RAF in '61 
and started coding vendor invoices to be punch-carded for a Power Samas 
tabulator. Moved with Unigate to Yeovil whee I became the Deputy 
Accountant - - my job, produce the internal month operating statements - 
Sales and Profit/Loss. Mid '63 - switched to systems analyst.

The only relevance to the above is that I'm trying to highlight why back 
in those days as computer people we didn't need to ask other 
bean-counters for advice. Between the three of us 'we had it covered'. 
Not so now, you MUST involve end-users in your project.

Back to the sales people. Absolutely green to computing our directive 
was 'Computerize Invoicing'; the Sales Ledger/Accounts Receivable would 
be Phase II - sometime...... Incredible FUN, we had to research, design 
and put together Product lists, how we were going to store customers' 
names and addresses etc. Of course the directive 'Computerize 
Invoicing', naturally implied a price look-up against the Product Catalogue.

Marketing foods - sales promotions. You discount your evaporated milk to 
beat Libbys or Carnation this week. Oops ! Next week it is Libbys that 
has a sales promotion, so in quick order Marketing dreams up another 
promotion. Anyway, that implies some flags set against specific products 
doesn't it. Then they also get innovative, let's offer this fortnight a 
discount on all our products which have the name "Farmer's Wife" on the 
label. A month later, let's have a promotion on products labeled 'Cow & 
Gate". With their blessing, (Green Giant), some three months later a 
promotion on "Green Giant" - back then we were their distributors as 
they were establishing their name in Europe.

Above you can see is getting a 'leetle' complicated, but not 
insurmountable, even with the old mainframe clunker we were using, and 
even though we were brand new to the game. (Machine was an ICT 1500, 
subsequently replaced by the ICL 1900 series. Richard is well acquainted 
with both).

So just to finish this pricing topic off, Geoff (as the ex-AR man), says 
"I wonder". So he gets his former cohorts to do a manual analysis of 
handwritten invoices coming in, either done by those van-salesmen, 
(driver and salesman) above, or the larger distribution depots clerical 
staff on behalf of their sales team. The analysis is done for one month. 
TWO THOUSAND variations from the standard published prices ! "Game over 
!", sez we. "No bloody way are we going to try and do price look-ups - 
it's unmanageable, and the net result from the ensuing cock-up - we 
three can expect to get a pink slip !".

Whether they were ever able to rationalize pricing to achieve price 
look-ups, I don't know. I left in '67, (exiting Trowbridge, Wiltshire, 
where the computer was based) - career move and more MONEY, back to my 
beloved Somerset to Debenhams (department store group) in the county 
town Taunton.

Whatever they particularly did then, (Unigate now no longer exists), or 
their successors, I don't need to tell you how fiercely competitive it 
is to-day, in the Americas or Europe, for a distributor to sell to 
supermarkets. The distributors have to be FLEXIBLE. Hello Micro Focus - 
"Flexibility ?".

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------
```

OK latest update :-

We can bitch and grind but finally you have to take the bull by the 
horns. Some ten minutes back e-mailed Tony Hill direct, and the last 
line reads :-

 > How's that for short -  two pages  ?  Now to test your flexibility 
and > 'open door policy'.


Keep you posted. Judson e-mail me and I'll send you a copy. YOUR EYES 
ONLY - no quotes.

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------

That's me out of c.l.c. again back to the real world of coding COBOL - 
correction - OO COBOL ! But I did think this topic was important for us 
M/F developers and hopefully it finishes as a win-win for both us and 
Micro Focus. Much too much drivel in c.l.c. Want a tip if you are using 
something like Mozilla; use the Google feature to view comp.lang.cobol 
which will show the very latest poster per thread. Then you can ignore 
the crap topics.

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------

Wiggy - my apologies Simon, I should have acknowledged with thanks your 
tip in the Forum on connecting to different DBs; but first an example 
from David Sands, quite a while back. Must make sure that also fits in. 
David had a DECLARE for the individual table, which in my case I will 
build into a class per table.

*>----------------------------------------------------------------------

Judson : What was it 'COBOL Work' - your rant and rave about OO. Only 
picked up on it because I saw a message from Richard. Michael W. chided 
you and Richard followed with "That's silly.......", and to my mind 
succinctly demonstrated the template approach which I strongly advocate.

My response would have been you are a bloody Luddite ;-). As I've said 
before, I stand more chance of getting you to attend a Latin mass at the 
Vatican than I do of converting you to OO. (Getting the other Baptist to 
the Vatican - might stand more chance getting Daniel there because he 
does believe in OO). I understand what you were saying about not looking 
at other classes ENCAPSULATION. Strictly speaking that's not true. But 
why bother to explain it to you here - you aren't interested anyway.

*>----------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-18T08:30:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ylff.6081$i7.4@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <CIdff.525673$1i.200184@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> Judson : What was it 'COBOL Work' - your rant and rave about OO. Only 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> bother to explain it to you here - you aren't interested anyway.

Actually, I am a Pentecostal. ;-)

Over the years I've done production work in more than 40 computer languages 
and dialects (many of them proprietary), and all but a few I learned on my 
own initiative. I do not oppose new things or innovation, I usually embrace 
them. Over the years I have had many 'gut reactions' to new concepts and 
technologies. Historically, they have served me well. For example, decision 
tables received a 'gut thumbs down', but structured programming received a 
'gut thumbs up'. I began as a typical spaghetti coder, but when first 
exposed to structured techniques, I immediately jumped on the bandwagon with 
great enthusiasm. As programming manager I led the charge, persuading my 
boss to implement a 'structured only' programming standard in our 
department, dragging all the programmers along, kicking and screaming. But 
my initial and continuing reaction to OO has been a resounding 'gut thumbs 
down'. I believe that tools should be only as complex as necessary, and 
always as simple as possible. OO just doesn't fit - it is overly complex 
from the get-go. OO is simply too complex, too non-intuitive, to be a 
*general solution*. Not that OO is bad, I just see OO as a solution for 
certain classes of problem, not a replacement for traditional procedural 
techniques, across the board. I have often made the analogy between OO vs. 
procedural and recursive vs. iterative, and I think it is very apt. 
Theoretically, anything that can be done iteratively can be done 
recursively, and vice-versa. The same holds with OO and procedural. Some 
recursive solutions have a wonderful elegance and simplicity, even beauty, 
about them. But no one would suggest replacing iterative with recursive 
across the board. I firmly believe that this is *precisely* the situation 
with OO vs. procedural, but the scale and order of the analogy is so much 
broader that it simply isn't obvious to everyone.

Do you remember the KISS principle? OO and KISS are mutually inconsistent, 
in concept, structure, learning curve and application. Just because it works 
and seems 'nifty gee-whiz' and tickles your logical creativity doesn't mean 
it's better. And just because it's popular doesn't mean it's better. 
Remember VHS/Betamax? People are actually using C derivative languages to 
write business applications. To me, this is insanity, similar to using COBOL 
to write a word processor. (Don't get me wrong, I like C for certain 
things - download BIGCALC from my website.)

I have and do continue to look at OO, and plan to get into OO more, by 
reason of necessity if nothing else. But my opinions on OO are hardly just 
mine. The associate who worked with me on that complex vehicle license 
system I've described here has come to a similar conclusion quite on his 
own, and he is very much into the newer stuff. He is highly intelligent, and 
has a scientific background. He has a BS in physics and an MS in CS and has 
taught CS at university level. He worked as a computer scientist in and 
around NASA for 20 years before joining me on the vehicle license system in 
'96. He did some development work on the "Star Wars" space defense missile 
tracking system, improving the tracking algorithm speed by an order of 
magnitude. He has studied and worked with OO more than I have. Like me, he 
believes OO is an unnecessary complication for most applications. Maybe I 
can persuade him to participate in this thread.

Perhaps it's because I have developed a set of highly effective procedural 
methodologies, or maybe it's because of the type of applications I 
encounter; but I have yet to see OO do anything that makes me want to jump 
on the bandwagon or witness any development effort where OO made a positive 
difference. Quite the reverse, in fact. Very much the reverse.

I know that once people master some difficult and/or esoteric skill, they 
often become very enamored of it, quite apart from the particular practical 
merits of the skill. It makes them feel special and exclusive, and this can 
be very seductive, even addictive. This is a well known, common 
psychological phenomenon that affects all of us. Considering the actual 
practical impact of OO that I have witnessed, I can't help but wonder if 
there is this kind of psychological motivation behind at least some of the 
OO enthusiasm I see? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-11-21T18:16:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <CIdff.525673$1i.200184@pd7tw2no> <8ylff.6081$i7.4@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
When I first heard about OO, I thought it would turn out to be as important 
an advance as Structured.   I *assumed* that OO, like Structured, had a 
mathematical foundation full of provably correct theorems.

Not being a mathemetician or a computer scientist, I didn't expect that I 
would ever fully understand the theory, but knowing that a theoretical basis 
existed would have motivated me to embrace OO as avidly as I had Structured 
a few years earlier.  (I am a reformed spaghetti-coder).  It turns out that 
there is no 'theory'.  OO is a descriptive discipline, more akin to biology 
than mathematics.

Here is a link that I enjoy visiting.  It is a bit polemical, but he seems 
to make sense:  http://www.geocities.com/tablizer/oopbad.htm

Another favourite:  http://www.dbdebunk.com/index.html This one is on the 
topic of relational data bases, which like Structured programming, is based 
on sound mathematical principles.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-22T06:06:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Izygf.564963$oW2.194316@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <CIdff.525673$1i.200184@pd7tw2no> <8ylff.6081$i7.4@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Don Leahy wrote:
> When I first heard about OO, I thought it would turn out to be as important 
> an advance as Structured.   I *assumed* that OO, like Structured, had a 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don,

Took a quick look at your first reference. Some of it is true and some 
items are pure Bullshit. The guy is either tongue-in-cheek or in 'Attack 
Mode'.

Progression of OO :

Simula--->Smalltalk--->Apple----->Windows

Simplification with many other languages introduced. Smalltalk like its 
predecessor was intended primarily as a Simulator, then the Xerox PARC 
team got cute producing a word processor where you could jump around the 
text in graphic mode; secondly without company blessing the then young 
Adele Goldberg with a criminal associate nipped some computers out the 
front door and whisked them off to a kindergarten for the kids to play 
with. They loved the graphics and intuitively became the first computer 
literate generation.

As he says, many OOPLs are used for GUI-ing; that does appear to be the 
emphasis when you look at Newsgroups for C or Java. But this only a 
fraction of its potential use - granted it's currently a big fraction.
Unlike us in COBOL, I wonder whether the upcoming generations in those 
languages understand business savvy. Comp.lang.object is a big offender.
If you are cross-reading messages, Chuck's explanation of how ISO/ANSI 
works - then he simplifies it with his plain English version.

That's not to say there aren't any who are conversant with 'bizzness' 
but there does seem to be a tendency to wrap it up in jargonese - making 
it difficult to decypher for newcomers.

Someone will ask, "Explain Multi Inheritance". He'll get back a 
dissertation on the ins and outs. Not all OO languages have 
Multi-Inheritance but COBOL does. Can't help but feel the attitude was, 
well some of the others do, so perhaps we had better have it as well.
Now if you have to do handstands and cartwheels to justify using Multi 
Inheritance with COBOL, then simply you are in the wrong zone. If with a 
flash of brilliance you can definitely use it, then go for it. But to 
artificially use it because it is there, it is just dead, dead wrong.

I was whisking through and saw his reference to inheritance : Class C 
inherits from Class B which in turn inherits from Class A. To keep it 
nice and obtuse I think he was writing about Cats or Dogs. (I don't 
think there are too many of us producing applications for 
veterinarians). Correctly he claims there are abuses, and without 
reading in depth, was that his justification for questioning the 
validity of Inheritance.

I use it all the time, but so far at only two levels in conjunction with 
the word REUSE - which is another term he takes a crack at.

I have complete access to the hierarchy of classes that Micro Focus 
provides, and if necessary I can step through them with the debugger 
(Animator TM). Rough check, five levels between Class Base and Classes 
Ordered or SortedCollection. Each sub-class drawing features from a 
super class up through that hierarchy. Chances are they may have spent 
perhaps 2 or 5 years figuring it all out - but it is comprehensive.

People blow a gasket if you start making attachments here, so some 
written examples of my own usage.

Class EditCustomers, might want to display a Lisbox or Listview of 
current customers. Similarly might need a Combo Dropdown list of 
county/province/states; or a list of countries. All entail a collection. 
To create those lists I'm going to need to reference the CustomerFile or 
for convenience a Description file for the dropdowns.

So based on Will Price's initial thoughts we have, and using his term 
DBI (DataBase Interface) whether we are talking COBOL files or DBs 
accessed with SQL :-

(a) EditCustomers (inherits from Base)
(b) Descriptions DBI (inherits from DBI Main)
(c) DBI Main (inherits from Base)
(d) either a DescripsFile or a DescripsTable

I want to create either a dictionary or collection - "Now what is the 
syntax to do that ? Ahh... Got it in another class/program - copy and 
paste and amend the wording". No so - I've put all my collection 
creation in DBI Main - sending a fixed set of parameters from Descrips 
DBI. DBI Main does a validity check on parameters and if there's a foul 
up an ANSI display " You've blown it", followed by STOP RUN. I don't 
need to get fancy with a GUI message - I'm testing and my source needs 
to be changed in DescripsDBI.

Having created the empty Collection object, it is returned to 
DescripsDBI to method "makeAcollection", bearing in mind we are talking 
solely 'Descriptions' - so either the method uses "readNext" against the 
COBOL file or for the Table CURSOR selection with SQL to return 
retrieved data to the same method. It is only DescripsDBI that knows the 
element format, Pic x(xxx) whatever. However as appropriate, subject to 
changes, regardless of the list I am using, I invoke super (DBI Main) to 
"addRecord", "changeRecord" "deleteRecord" etc.

Three Tier System design. I taught myself COBOL, fortunately didn't do 
too bad a job and from Dan McCracken's first on "Structure Programming" 
managed to dodge spaghetti-coding. Back in Micro Focus Compuserve days 
picked up from Pete Dashwood the phrase "Three Tier System Design"; cute 
enough to pick up what he was saying, applied it also to my OO approach :-

- Business Logic
- User Interface (GUI-ing)
- DBI - DataBase Interface - as per above

As part of sending parameters to DBI Main, DescripsDBI also sends the 
'positioning' of fields in elements applicable to this 'Instance' = the 
DescripsDBI collection. User clicks on a Listbox, Droplist whatever. Two 
ways of going about that, (a) Can be done in the logic for the Dialog or 
(b) from the event identify an element index position in the collection.
I think option (b) sits better with the concept of Three Tier Design above.

So the event sends the request back to CustomerEdit. That in turn via 
sub-class DesripsDBI is invoking DBIMain to retrieve the collection 
object getting at the appropriate element by the index number - much 
like you would want to get an element from a Working-Storage table 
occurs "a" to "b" depending on "c". So I have quite a few methods in 
DBIMain which are vanilla flavoured, getting objects, establishing the 
record PrimeKey I'm after etc..., applicable to ANY collection I create.

If I'm accessing 60 files or Tables the DBI Main is applicable to ALL. 
For the sub-classes like DescripsDBI then I take a copy of one, re-name 
it and change specifics - but the donkey work is done in DBI Main. Just 
double-checked, Descrips DBI has its own 12 methods, Materials DBI 11 
and DBI Main has 23. Descrips DBI and Materials DBI; their methods 
result from the copying above, a slant on copy/paste.

Obviously many different approaches to this, but that's the solution 
I've arrived at. A perfectly legitimate use of the inheritance hierarchy 
plus REUSE.

Backtrack on message where I first mention (a) CustomerEdit - there's 
another leg, I need at least one GUI (Customer Interface) or perhaps 
more, depending upon what I'm doing.

For starters there is a finite group of Windows Controls, plus a finite 
group of APIs associated with GUI-ing - no matter how you approach it, 
with whichever tool, your GUI code finishes up calling a specific API. 
Some calls/invokes are applicable to particular controls; other like 
"hide", "disable" "enable" etc., can be applied against a large group of 
the controls.

Looking at the Micro Focus demo apps and use of controls I could quite 
easily have done a 'copy and paste' of their code, changing as 
necessary, and then for each Dialog generated, thorough testing. That is 
real tedious - somebody in S. Africa as some 1,000 controls, "granted 
he's using Dialog System and not Dialog Editor, like I am - but 
nevertheless extremely tedious and a lot of repetitive code.

You'll get a clue what I'm doing here if you look at my reply to Alain's 
"Passing Parameters".

(a) EditCustomers (inherits from Base)
(b) TabbedDialog ( as opposed to say an Enter key dialog) inherits from
     Class Modal
(c) CustomersDialog (inherits from TabbedDialog) - this is the extract I
     included in the message to Alain

1. CustomersEdit - invoke TabbedDialog "new" (well actually "start" for 
this one) returning os-Dialog

2. CustomerEdit - invoke CustomersDialog "new" using os-Dialog returning 
TemporaryObject ( so it now has a handle to its parent os-Dialog - which 
is the actual Customer-Dialog we see on the screen)

3. Invoke TemporaryObject "begin" - where it now passes the parameter 
list, line by line to os-Dialog to create a specific Dialog - this 
'Instance' being Customer-Dialog. Finished with TemporaryObject can 
'finalize" or destroy - gonzo !

4. Now CustomerEdit takes over and tells os-Dialog to display itself, 
"show". User clicks on the Listbox the index position in in the 
collection is returned to EditCustomers - then we follow through with 
what I explained about DescripsDBI and DBI Main.

In this second leg what I've attempted is with a template use of one 
class to react to all methods required for the various controls I use.
So far it has worked out. Treeviews - you couldn't possibly contain the 
logic for one of those in a Dialog template, but it is the Dialog 
Template that picks up on Treeview events, returning them to say 
CustomerEdit or another Driver, which in turn passes the events to 
Levels within your Treeview control logic.

REUSE again - I think this thread - pick up on my reply to Dnald about 
using vanilla flavoured classes to handle COBOL files.

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------

You author definitely has his feet in the bovine manure or rather like 
Tom Cruise, "Eyes Wide Shut". Cruise has turned out to be a bit of a 
bimbo - fancy giving up on a fascinating lady like Nicole Kidman who is 
also an excellent actress.


PS: If you are gettig into the seasonal mood, print off a copy of that 
reference, coloured of course, wrapped in a nice red bow and send it to 
Judson - he will be deeply appreciative :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:Izygf.564963$oW2.194316@pd7tw1no...
> As he says, many OOPLs are used for GUI-ing; that does appear to be the 
> emphasis when you look at Newsgroups for C or Java. But this only a 
> fraction of its potential use - granted it's currently a big fraction.
> Unlike us in COBOL, I wonder whether the upcoming generations in those 
> languages understand business savvy.

The business understands the business, the coder understands the code.
That's why there are careers as "Business Analysts", "Requirements 
Analysts", "Software Engineers".

The problem is not that the next generation doesn't understand 
business...they just don't understand anything that isn't a "pattern".

DU

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T06:05:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LETgf.576571$1i.111237@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<nsNgf.18495$dT3.2101@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <CIdff.525673$1i.200184@pd7tw2no> <8ylff.6081$i7.4@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com> <Izygf.564963$oW2.194316@pd7tw1no> <nsNgf.18495$dT3.2101@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
defaultuser wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:Izygf.564963$oW2.194316@pd7tw1no...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Firstly to the second paragraph - the odd peek at comp.lang.object and 
they (the 'regulars' providing sage advice), and newbies who have picked 
up a reference in some text book - both parties have a complete 
obsession with patterns. So many times, when it wasn't a coded C or Java 
example, but where the problem was being expressed in text, I wanted to 
jump in and say, "What's your problem. This is how I would do it in OO 
COBOL. Read in a file, do this, do that etc...". Of course first 
obstacle, "What file ?". No good talking about ISAMs, Relative etc., 
they've already lost it.

Now to the first paragraph - and without going to great lengths explain 
to me a short definition of Business Analysts, Requirements Analysts, 
Software Engineers. I've been so long removed from the mainframe world 
where this is only likely to occur. Base your comments on what follows :-

1963 - virtually nobody; OK there were comparatively a few sites in both 
N. America and European countries, but EDP/ADP was just starting to warm 
up. Recruits were needed and there was a very positive division of 
labour - analysts and programmers, exactly as the two words indicate.

Not completely so, but analysts were I think, primarily ex-bean counters 
with an accounting designation or perhaps studying for one. Unlike CAs, 
back then primarily auditing, these ex-bean-counters were 'doers' on the 
job double-entry stuff; supervisors or departmental heads.

Programmers - "Have we got some bright eyed, bushy tailed youngsters who 
  would like to take a look at programming ?"

That division more or less persisted until I came to Canada in 1975. 
(Oops clarification - I was talking about what went on in UK).

Back then, and has to be American terminology, we referred to Chief 
Systems Analyst, ("White man. Him speak with forked-tongue"). So analyst 
meant everything except the coding/programming - politician, salesman, 
(wise guy in some instances), probing (analytical), form designing, 
writing procedural manuals, perhaps a hand in selection of some 
equipment, say OCR or OMR back then. He or a team conceptually designed 
the application, lots of scribbling and wipe-outs on whiteboards until 
the thing crystalized.

Now, to a formalized document standard, program specifications were 
produced, a broad flowchart giving you a snap shot of the computer, 
(centred) indicating ins and outs on paper tape/cards/OCR/OMR and 
naturally to mag tapes or disks (well intially RCA's Magnetic Card File, 
but disks on my next move), and of course oodles of printed reports. 
Included in the document specification were records-in, records-out and 
file design. Validation checks and error procedures.

When the application went live, analysts/team leaders took to nursing 
the users through it.

Naturally career-wise those young programmers wanted to go up and up, 
even if although incredible coders, they didn't have what it takes to be 
an analyst. Firstly of course they don't know the 'business' although 
the smarter ones could pick up what it was about based on the 
programming they were asked to do, and if they were sitting in on any 
meetings wit users. Now while there can be no definition of 'Prince 
Charming' for an analyst, nevertheless you have got to strike 
credibility from the lowest peon, conveying to them, they count as much 
as the Big White Chief, to make the applicaiton work, and perhaps in 
some instances reaching out to some geriatric on the Board of Directors.

I moved companies in '67 initially as an analyst, became eventually 
Systems Manager and roughly a year before coming to Canada in '75, 
Systems and Programming Manager. So as Systems Manager I had no direct 
input to the following - it occurred before I became the head of both. 
To create a career path, the Chief Programmer pressed for a Mainteance 
Programmers' Group. The real bright ones who could do quick fixes to get 
us out of a glitch that the lesser trained had created.

Next step was to extend that career path yet again. From the Maintenance 
Programmers' Group now create a Quality Control Group. They acted like 
omsbudsmen to gripes from the Operations Manager where he claimed 
running a particular application was tortuously slow, a pain in the butt 
because of its complexity etc. Now the problem could be either 
'analytical' or 'programming' - so they reviewed; where necessary they 
had the authority to change the simpler things - their own small team of 
programmers who could do a fix. Obviously if 'analytical' then they hit 
me with the problem, "This application is just dead lousy". So perhaps 
some fair discussion and resolution - not that it occurred, but that was 
a possibility.

Kind of fed up with the whole English scene I made it for Canada in '75 
direct to Calgary where my younger brother had been here for some ten 
years. The Downtown core was stinkin' rich because of the oil patch. 
(Same again right now, times TEN or times ONE HUNDRED and numbers are 
always written in $BILLIONS because of the HUGE potential of the 
Athabasca Tar Sands - gooey oil mixed with sand. VP Dick Cheyney was 
scheduled for a come and look see prior to Katrina and New Orleans).

Corporately my brother had an 'official' office but his main office was 
his favourite watering-hole where the lunch tab back then was $150 for a 
meagre sandwich shared by all and probably 12 gallons of booze.

I landed in this fuzzy drink-laden world. Strike # 1- I wasn't aware, 
but at 45 the 'Grey Hair syndrome" was already in play. With that grey 
coloured hair, couldn't possibly know what IT was about - and no doubt, 
just occasionally, "This guy probably knows more than I do". Strike # 2 
- seems they didn't want analysts at all. Subsequent reading of 
newspaper ads revealed the words 'analyst' and 'programmer' as a pair - 
analyst/programmer, programmer/analyst and some real cute innovations. 
Somewhere along the line - the two jobs could be done by one person.

Now it seems to me that perhaps for a few years now, or perhaps a decade 
or so, the role of 'analyst' has taken on fresh significance ? So what 
do your terms above embrace ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T23:49:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ulk6aF125tskU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <NTsdf.494865$tl2.408876@pd7tw3no> <CIdff.525673$1i.200184@pd7tw2no> <8ylff.6081$i7.4@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
 

"Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:Czsgf.995$e43.132429@news20.bellglobal.com...
>
> When I first heard about OO, I thought it would turn out to be as 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> akin to biology than mathematics.

If there was no biology, there would be no mathematics.

Why would you embrace something because it had a mathematical basis, even 
though you didn't understand it? Isn't that a bit like believing in God 
because you trust the Church?

Now, a high priest tells you it has no mathematical basis so you consider it 
worthless.

Would you deny God because a priest told you He doesn't exist? (Assuming you 
were a Believer in the first place, of course).

Not everything of value has a mathematical basis.

The fact is that OO is used every day by people who have no idea it even 
exists, when they switch on their computers and their Operating Systems and 
invoke methods of encapsulated objects instantiated from OO classes... It 
doesn't need maths to make it work.

I long ago gave up evangelizing OO in this forum (or anywhere else, 
actually) but I can say that after being a programmer for 40 years, there 
are 2 milestones that stand out in my memory:

1. Understanding what Dyjstrka was on about, and changng my coding habits 
accordingly.
2. The blinding flash of insight that gave me the realization that OO was 
the key to components, and components were the keys to the Kingdom.

Both these events changed my view of programming.  One led to me being more 
productive, the other made me learn Java and OO COBOL and start building 
components, which revolutionized and empowered the whole way I develop 
systems. With this technology I have done things I could never have done 
without it (Web based services for a start, but that's just one thing.) The 
growing library of reusable components that can be dropped on a Windows 
desktop or on a web page with no change, maintenance free, encapsulated 
pieces of functionality, that can be assembled like Lego blocks into 
structures of grace and elegance.

Mathematics is a fine and wonderful science. But so is biology....

Pete.
```

Probably not, but that is not my argument.

Your definitions are not the ones I used.

I was using "biology" as a collective noun for living things. Admittedly, a 
loose use, but I believe acceptable.
(If I was to talk about the biology of a planet as being predatory, I don't 
think anyone would misunderstand the usage).

I was using "mathematics" = the science of calculation, numeracy, the 
popularly accepted meaning of the term.

If there were no living things, there would be no mathematics, because it 
was formulated and conceived by living organisms.

As for leaving philosophising until I retire...

1. Why would I take the advice of some character in an obscure movie? (It 
may be a "Classic" in your universe, but it is unheard of in mine..)
2. Why would I change the habit of a lifetime? :-)

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

- **From:** "charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-13T09:57:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
I agree with you.  When I retire I want to write programs that I want to 
write, and I had plans to provide algorithms, tools and utilities for COBOL. 
I was shocked and amazed at the prices charged for COBOL compilers. COBOL is 
much less complex than C++ yet it costs so much more for a compiler.  They 
are not interested in individual sales and small companies. In one thread 
they mentioned having to provide highly functional IDE's for companies. I 
just do not buy their argument. There was a guy writing books and magazine 
articles on C++ and he cobbled together a pretty good IDE for use with his 
book in just a few months.

I admit that at one time I could not afford a COBOL compiler, but now even 
though I could afford one I will not pay their price. I also agree that it 
is their right to choose their market and how to approach it, but it is my 
right to go in another direction as well.

Java is free and a seems to be a good language and has a lot of very 
powerful classes for a very wide range of application types.  My initial 
response to Java a few years ago was somewhat negative but I now believe 
that was mostly due to a sleep apnea problem which affected my learning 
process.  Since I have resolved that problem both Java and OO programming in 
general have seemed much easier to learn.

The point is not to switch to Java but to simply realize that other 
alternatives are out there if you are open to learning some new things and 
learning new things is one of the main reasons I got involved with computers 
and programming in the first place.

I have felt my own version of you pain over this matter and I hope you find 
an acceptable solution to resolve your situation.



"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
>I feel very strongly about this issue, and I wanted to see how you other 
>COBOL users feel about it.
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Well I am not up to date on the 2002 specification because I figure I will 
be retired before any vendor implements it, so I cannot comment about the 
complexity of it.  Does anyone know of any vendor who is actually trying to 
implement it? It seems to me that vendors have been adding their own 
enhancements in the absence of timely standards, some/many of which may now 
conflict with the 2002 standard. They are probably unwilling to undo what 
they have done and lose upward compatibility.  Of course they could do it by 
adding more compiler options.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-14T18:42:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_T4ef.143118$Io.69747@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <11nf4277aisd16c@corp.supernews.com> <1c896$4377e65d$4f9c60e$30291@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
"charles hottel" <jghottel@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
news:1c896$4377e65d$4f9c60e$30291@DIALUPUSA.NET...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
> news:11nf4277aisd16c@corp.supernews.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> to implement it?

    I'm working on something similar to a COBOL compiler (eighty-something 
(84?), not 2002). I've also worked on a Java compiler back in university, 
and I must say the COBOL compiler is much, much, much more complicated. 
Perhaps on the order of 3 or 4 times more complex.

    COBOL's "noise words" (e.g. "OF", "IN", etc.) are somewhat of an 
annoyance, and the "abbreviated conditionals" feature in particular makes 
the grammar non-context-free, which means most "modern" tools for compiler 
development either won't work at all, or need to be heavily hacked to get 
them to work with COBOL. Almost every programming language developped these 
days can be parsed with a context-free parser, but not COBOL.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-14T19:44:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rO5ef.498313$oW2.247555@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1c896$4377e65d$4f9c60e$30291@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <11nf4277aisd16c@corp.supernews.com> <1c896$4377e65d$4f9c60e$30291@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
charles hottel wrote:
> Well I am not up to date on the 2002 specification because I figure I will 
> be retired before any vendor implements it, so I cannot comment about the 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> adding more compiler options.

How soon are you retiring ? :-) In all probability your statement about 
implementation is correct, plus the enhancements. From your perspective 
writing utilities, as a hobby, and perhaps you want to market - purely 
aimed at (a) Procedural COBOL or (b) the Web-world which implies 
OO-ing/GUI-ing/Web-ing.

Just to take OO alone :-

- IBM - not much bothered; they provide the basics for generating OO 
COBOL classes BUT, in Enterprise, "MyClass inherits from JavaBase" - 
Java being responsible for creating/destroying objects - auto garbage 
collection. Will IBM ever implement the TR(Technical Report) on 
Collections/Dictionaries - why should they, there's a another version of 
that in Java.

- Unisys - noticed any hint from Chuck that they are interested ?
- HP/Compaq - ditto
- Liant (RM/COBOL) - ditto
- Acu COBOL - ditto
- Realia - ditto
- Fujitsu-Siemens - I'm reasonably sure Karl is interested, seeing as he 
keeps a watching brief on comp.lang.object.

- Fujitsu - Possibly closet to what became 2002 - they came in after 
IBM,(Visual Age), Hitachi and Micro Focus already had taken a crack 
based on the early musings (around '96) before COBOL 2002 became a 
reality. I would suggest their version of collections/dictionaries is 
closest to the TR(Technical Report) from J4 on lists. Still, they went 
the dotNet route - so you can use any other language for lists.

- Micro Focus - (along with Hitachi) - collections/dictionaries based on 
the Smalltalk model. OO certainly has the J4 2002 model, but I wont 
guarantee exactly every feature, without checking - which I'm not going 
to do :-). In their OO model not only can a sub-class inherit from super 
class, but the sub-class can be made to inherit the Global data from 
super class as well. Translation - wihout having to :-

invoke super "getTheSortFlag" returning SortFlag

Given that global copyfile feature above, you can, in the sub-class, 
directly code  :-

Evaluate true
	when SortFlag ......
	when Sortflag ......
	when etc....
End-Evaluate

So before 2002 that, of several OO features, was a Micro Focus 
'extension'. Now that we have 2002 that feature is still an 'extension'.

Given the references aboove to who is actually providing OO and who in 
fact are currently providing colllections/dictionaries, leads me to the 
question - "For whom are J4 producing the TR on Collections ?"

Clear as mud, isn't it ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-13T22:20:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET>`

```
charles hottel wrote:
> I agree with you.  <snip>

Charles,

Nice to hear from you again. Here's a sobering thought and had to go dig 
down in the basement to get actual figures from Feb '81. Canadian dollars :-

- Hardware :

Tandy Radio Shack Model II - 64 K  ($5,645)
1 Drive Extension (10 inch floppies) ($1,699)
Line Printer VI ($1,350)
Extras (power bar, Printer cable, cover etc) ($193)

- Software :

Tandy's SCRIPSIT word processor ($379)
RM/COBOL ( '74) ($399).

Hardware : $8,694	Software : 778

RM/COBOL was bare bones compiler - no editor, examples, tips etc. Hence 
the reason for SCRIPSIT to act as an editor. The COBOL compiler did the 
job. Slowness was purely from the 'primitive' machine being used.

I guess those hardware and software figures have got somewhat switched 
around.

In a hobbyist vein, it just doesn't make sense for you to shell out for 
a commercial COBOL compiler. If and when they get around to OO perhaps, 
KOBOL or one of the other 'Open Source' compilers might do it for you.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no>`

James J. Gavan wrote:
> charles hottel wrote:
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Why would any person "make do" with a bit of fluff when there are dozens
of powerfull compilers and compiler tools available?

To set up and compile with Cobol costs thousands of dollars, and it is
only going to be one of a dozen tools required to do the job. When you
are done, it costs each person that wishes to use it hundreds of dollars
ti *use your product*.  I want to sell the software I write, not design
loss leaders for a compiler company.

I am curently making a good living converting code from Cobol to other
languages, because even the people that own it do not want to be in that
position. Cobol is now one of a dozen languages, and about a decade and
a half behind all of them. Yet they want to charge double for their
product. I simply can no longer make a profit if I use the language.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-14T00:45:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Why would any person "make do" with a bit of fluff when there are dozens
> of powerfull compilers and compiler tools available?

I can certainly understand your irritance, but a "bit of fluff" ?
I can't comment on Fujitsu but I think it is fairly comparative to Net 
Express in that it does have comprehensive included features. Bearing in 
mind you took the route of using Flexus SP2 for screening, there's one 
of your first additional costs.

So without having the 'latest greatest' NE V4, (still using V3.1), I have :-

(a) My preference, an integrated Development Environment for everything

(b) File handling feature, which I no longer use. And you know what you 
get if you try to open a comp-3 file with say MS Word - bugger all ! 
Accidentally tried to open an ISAM, and their Data File Editor shows the 
file with the comp-3s as Usage Display.

(c) Two choices of GUIs :-

(i) Dialog System ( which has come a longgggggg way since the DOS days - 
again I don't use)

(ii) Dialog Editor and GUI classes.

(iii) Well there are other choices for GUIs - you can hook into Java or 
C; for Java certainly classes to 'communicate' with Java, and I'm 
talking V 3.1 not V 4 with dotNet. Interestingly Calin there in Toronto 
recently asked about some White Paper. Appears you can use V 3.1 to do a 
kinda dotNet approach, (haven't read it yet).

(d) SQL - You can jump right in without literally any expertise, 
although it makes sense to bone up on the topic a bit. The ESQL 
Assistant generates your copyfiles to handle the DB format and the 
interpreted COBOL formats. Better still - set up a Query in the ESQL 
Assistant - you can run the query and get results without compiling one 
line of COBOL. Those same queries can be copy/pasted into your COBOL source.

(e) I'm sure there may be other features which I may not yet have 
discovered. There's something to support XML - but I think that is in V 4.0.

For my money above is a pretty nifty set of features.

I'm not defending their pricing, but the accumulation of the above 
appeals to me. Be assured I much preferred the $800 for VISOC as opposed 
to some $3,500 for Net Express. Water under the bridge now, but we got 
ROYALLY screwed for the move from VISOC to N/E. I would guess that some 
moved to Fujitsu. I know Calin took a serious look, but didn't opt for 
that change.

Because of a cock-up on their database around the Merant disaster, I 
didn't get reminded that my annual Technical fee, (around $1,000) was 
due. So if I wanted to upgrade to N/E V 4.0 I'm absolutely screwed. 
According to their rules I'm a 'newbie' and would have to pay the full 
price all over again.

Technical support - well they have several innovative schemes, 'money 
makers' and an annual licence for Tech Support - the latter is in effect 
letting  you buy the next Version on a installment payment method; you 
get interim Fixpacks and enhancements and are entitled to automatically 
upgrade to the next Version they issue. (The more advanced Tech Support 
is where the larger organizations have a real nutty problem that can't 
be solved).

The other leg of the Tech Support is what I used to refer to as Answer 
Exchange. Alan Wheeler who administered the old Compuserve Forum dreamed 
up that name but brought me up to date calling it 'Micro Fcous Forum'. 
That's  the one I used to keep referring folks to. It's a freebie. 
Either developers or M/F staff can jump in with suggestions. It's still 
the old, old problem - Windoze. People, primarily using Dialog System, 
seeking clarification or being fed back with an API coded solution to 
their particular problem. Additionally solutions are provided for 
'talking' to C or Java.

Runtime Fees. Now that really is a different baby, and a stinker ! I 
can't offer any solutions, but if Judson provides some ammo on what the 
costs are to him then I can certainly make at least one suggestion. So I 
await his reply.
> 
> To set up and compile with Cobol costs thousands of dollars, and it is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> loss leaders for a compiler company.

Bit emotive above Donald. Exactly what costs are you referring to. Care 
to spell it out with an example  ?
> 
> I am curently making a good living converting code from Cobol to other
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Well truly, success on your conversions. And overall I can understand 
why you got pissed off.

......I await Judson's reply on the runtime fees. Typical of c.l.c., 
somebody asks a question and the thread goes off at a tangent on 
divergent topics. Somebody asks about how you do something with SQL and 
we finish up discussing the size of Marilyn Monroe's breasts :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-13T21:12:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aoSdf.728$w84.94181@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com> <%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You were suggesting that we forgo a compiler like net express, and go
with one like Kobol.  Kobol is not a serious tool. I will grant that
both Fujitsu and MF are full fledged compilers, and was not refering to
either of them as a bit of fluff.  I was refering to what you suggested
as a replacement. Kobol is not mature enough to write anything serious
in, and I doubt it ever will be. If I cannot use one of the "real" Cobol
compilers, then I will go to another language.  There are dozens of them.

>>
>> To set up and compile with Cobol costs thousands of dollars, and it is
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Run time fees. In fact, I can not even give a customer *a free copy* of
my software. I have to pay a price, depending on the number of terminals
the customer has, to allow them to try it out free. If I try to sell it
on the net, allowing them to download a trial copy, then I would be
sued, under the terms they wish me to sign.

That means that I cannot produce any product worth less than several
thousand dollars, that uses Cobol. The reason for that is not just the
cost of the fee, but also the cost of the marketing.  In fact, to even
demonstrate the system, I have to either travel to the customer site or
bring the customer to my site. Figure $500 to get to a site in another
city, and give a demo, then get home again. If they are more than 400
miles away (95% of my customers), then double that. In an international
market, that pretty well rules out 99.999% of all software. If I charge
only $5000 per copy of my code, then I can afford to show it to about 5
people per sale. If only one in 10 buys, I am out of pocket. The norm is
1 in 20 or 30.

The bottom line is that Cobol can only be used on software worth $30,000
or so per install. I converted a two and a half million line Cobol
system to a 4GL system last year for the cost of less than double that.
A demo now costs an Email, say 5 cents. So, for the cost of two sales, I
can drop marketing costs to 1/2000th per prospect.  If 1 in 30 buys,
then the change in language cuts my marketing cost from $15,000.00 per
copy to $1.50 per copy.

>>
>> I am curently making a good living converting code from Cobol to other
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Half the problem is that they will not tell you the price.  They hum and
they haw until you have spent your money, and then discover you are
*!@#$ed. I was told that I could could package up a demo version and
give it away free, but it was on the phone, and I was given a flat
refusal when I asked for it in writing. In fact, I was told I had to
sign a document saying that I would not, and was liable if I did. *That*
was in writing.

Cobol is now something I do as a hobby, or on other people's dimes.

Donald
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
> ......I await Judson's reply on the runtime fees. Typical of c.l.c., 
> somebody asks a question and the thread goes off at a tangent on divergent 
> topics. Somebody asks about how you do something with SQL and we finish up 
> discussing the size of Marilyn Monroe's breasts :-)


Jimmy, I'm waiting until business hours today (Monday) so I can get 
specifics from the MF sales staff. Last time I looked, MF did not give 
actual costs on their web site. Can't say I blame them for that, considering 
the sticker shock. :-)  I don't remember how much the runtime fees are, but 
just as bad or worse is having to saddle my clients with a piece of buggy 
crap like the compiler licensing enforcement software, which is truly 
horrible! From time to time I go to run the compiler and the licensing 
software has become corrupted or simply decided I no longer am entitled to 
use the compiler. Assuming I can even get a fix without reinstalling 
Windows, I have to wait for that, and go through TWO cycles of response 
keys. And MF wants me to place my clients in that position? And what about a 
client who has been happily using, with no runtime fees, software I wrote 
for them 10 years ago and just because I make a slight change or bug fix, 
they now have to start paying runtime fees to MF? How am I supposed to 
justify that to a client? It is entirely unreasonable and unconscionable. A 
serious betrayal of a loyal customer base.

For anyone who thinks I am exaggerating the trouble I have had with 
licensing keys, see the log below. It is not entirely complete, because I 
became so disgusted during one series of problems that I quit posting to the 
log for a while.

- **In-Reply-To:** `<4378C0E5.BC88F4EF@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com> <%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no> <27_df.14132$kd.9637@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <4378C0E5.BC88F4EF@mts.net>`

```
Peter Lacey wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

"Earwigo again" said the earwig as it fell off the wall. What's your 
objective Peter, to prove my quote about Marilyn above was absolutely 
correct :-)

Ten years ago ? What were you looking at, the old DOS version or VISOC; 
the latter I'm sure would have had what I'm about to illustrate. I hate 
those on-line helps, but they also have on-line manuals, so one of the 
LRMs (Language Reference Manuals), mentioning Screen Section would 
probably take you straight to this.

Seeing as it's you and me, two ex-Brits - did it through the on-line 
help with a search on 'COLOUR' - every conceivable reference to COLOR 
you don't need until you get a bit cute. The immediate references refer 
to options for colorizing your IDE. Take a second shot at 
Foreground-Color :-

1.	This clause is available for use only with a color screen.
2.	Integer-1 or identifier-1 specifies the foreground color of the 
screen item. The colors and their corresponding values are:

0	black	8	grey
1	blue	9	light blue
2	green	10	light green
3	cyan	11	light cyan
4	red	12	light red
5	magenta	13	light magenta
6	brown or yellow	14	yellow
7	white	15	high intensity white

There you go - what's wrong with that ? Didn't bother to check for RGB 
used with GUI-ing, but I think the above is what you were referring to.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-18T21:49:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o%rff.189517$Ur3.117046@fe01.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com> <%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no> <27_df.14132$kd.9637@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <4378C0E5.BC88F4EF@mts.net> <T_4ef.502687$1i.418040@pd7tw2no>`

```
For variou reasons (including being away from home), I haven't posted to 
this thread.

I did want to make one point that I think *may* have been missed in this 
thread.

The current Micro Focus "business plan" seems TO ME to be targeted SOLELY at 
"enterprise customers".  To the extent "individual developers" are 
interested and want to purchase the product, that is fine - but it is NOT 
what they are aimed at.

The current comp.lang.cobol participation is PRIMARILY from "individual 
developers" and therefore, outside the group to which Micro Focus is selling 
their product (and product lines).

This has certainly NOT always been true. (I remember hte VER yold "COBOL" 
vs" TOOLSET vs Workbench product lines).  I also know about the history of 
the "doomed" "Ojbect COBOL product offering".

I certainly can understain the frustration of those individual (or small 
business) developers who have used Micro Focus products in the past.  If you 
feel "abandonnded" - then (personally) my sense is that this is an ACCURATE 
reflection.

On the other hand, (going to the actual "subject line" of this thread), when 
it comes to Enterprise Customers of the Micro Focus products, I think that 
they *are* meeting their needs (both in pricing and enhancments - and even 
in run-time licensing).

comp.lang.cobol people may not "like this" - but I see little evidence to 
indicate that:

A) there *is* a mjor marke tof any vendor to provide individual developer 
COBOL products and tools
B) Micro Focus is "suffering" from targeting the enterprise COBOL 
customer(s).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-19T06:57:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K1Aff.534967$tl2.179102@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<o%rff.189517$Ur3.117046@fe01.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com> <%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no> <27_df.14132$kd.9637@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <4378C0E5.BC88F4EF@mts.net> <T_4ef.502687$1i.418040@pd7tw2no> <o%rff.189517$Ur3.117046@fe01.news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> For variou reasons (including being away from home), I haven't posted to 
> this thread.
> 
> I did want to make one point that I think *may* have been missed in this 
> thread.

Well there is a simple answer to that one; it wasn't *missed*, rather we 
didn't *know". It was Tony's reply cc'd to John Billman BTW, that made 
me pick up on CORE business, (my capitalization). So I went back 
checking against the Financials :-

Without referencing again can't remember whether revenues were dollars 
or pounds (presumably dollars).

Millions and percentages respectively for year ending 2005 :-

Licensing  079.9 53.0
Maintenance 066.7 44.3
Consulting  004.1 02.7

Percentages comparable to y/e 2004. Small wonder they are happy with 
their CORE = 79.9 million
> 
> The current Micro Focus "business plan" seems TO ME to be targeted SOLELY at 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
That comes across in the financials.

> The current comp.lang.cobol participation is PRIMARILY from "individual 
> developers" and therefore, outside the group to which Micro Focus is selling 
> their product (and product lines).

True about folks commenting in c.l.c but not quite so clear in the M/F 
Forum. Some are certainly from bigger software houses, numbers not 
known. Probably a number of min-software house, 3 - 4 but definitely a 
fair number of 'singles' - Johnny lonesomes. The following is definitely 
from Johnny Lonsesome unless he works with three buddies who couldn't 
help him - they would have to be dimwits.

*>------------------------------------------------------------------
I have a problem with a sequential file which has variable records.
Header- 7 characters
Detail- 1000 characters.
---------
and further down in the message :-
---------
Before doing the WRITEs.
I do:
MOVE 'xx' TO RE010D2-HEADER-fin.
WRITE RE010D2-HEADER.

MOVE 'xx' TO RE010D2-DETALLE-fin.
WRITE RE010D2-DETALLE.

Remarks : where 'xx' are ALT 13 y ALT 10. CR/LF.

I can't use LINE SEQUENTIAL FILE because in the detail the last field 
has spaces and CR-LF MUST BE in the 999 and 1000 position(It's fixe).

My problem is:
The file generated doesn't have a variable record.
My question: How can I solve this ?
*>------------------------------------------------

Well we know he's talking about x"odoa" - but used with LINE SEQUENTIAL 
only. Not knocking him, just illustration of Johnny Lonesome struggling.

Quickly knocked up a test program using those template File classes I 
mentioned to Donald. Whether he will latch on or totally lose it, don't 
know. The kicker is your suggestion to me, way back, "Record varying 
from 'a' to 'b' depending upon 'c'". Although using your recommendation 
I was using against FIXED length records; little bit of thought, was 
able to change the classes very quickly to accept true FIXED and true 
VARIABLE in one class.

Above is a digression, but I get the impression lots of 'singles' in the 
Forum.

> 
> This has certainly NOT always been true. (I remember hte VER yold "COBOL" 
> vs" TOOLSET vs Workbench product lines).  I also know about the history of 
> the "doomed" "Ojbect COBOL product offering".
> 
Plus the first shaft of course VISOC at $800, "Welcome aboard". And then 
  "Welcome to the Cross and Bones Line" = Net Express at around 
#3,000-3,500. Still leaves a bad taste.

> I certainly can understain the frustration of those individual (or small 
> business) developers who have used Micro Focus products in the past.  If you 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I could continue this but see no point. However I would start an 
entirely new thread labeled, "Is COBOL doomed". This is not M/F per se, 
but all the vendors and J4. Watch Chuck bridle. It's largely the 
emphasis on Mainframe or the phrase "Legacy applications", and the 
inability to acknowledge variant operating systems in our Standards.

-----------------
Sidebar for a minute : Said to Don Schricker at Newbury in 2000, "liked 
your article in xxxxx about recognizing different O/S". "Huh ?", he 
replied, like I had just arrived from Mars - bearing in mind Don has a 
beard. Got back to Canada then twigged, Alan Saccardi M/F, also with a 
beard. Not in depth but he illustrated the ability within OO to trigger 
you were using Windoze, Linux or Unix etc. Then you call/invoke the 
appropriate classes.
------------------

In the short term (and I don't define that), fine. In the long term, I'm 
now even more less sure. With echoes of Carl Sagan "There are BILLIONS 
of lines coded in COBOL" we get from Gartner. Any stats anybody ? There 
are a million programmers (?) universally but the number using COBOL is 
..... and is decreasing by .... per year. Associated with that who is 
going to man the mainframes when the old sweats retire ?

> comp.lang.cobol people may not "like this" - but I see little evidence to 
> indicate that:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> customer(s). 

Well Judson, some very unpalatable decisions to make in your situation. 
I'm numbed by it but have to rationalize. Really I'm in hobbyist mode, 
but could theoretically try and flog my app - providing I came under the 
Amnesty Clause; however if I got the runaround because I didn't qualify 
for the 'Amnesty' and had to start asking M/F about runtime PRICE - 
seems to me, along with others, I should start looking at other languages.

If I was still 25-30,I think I would swiftly make a move to Java. I am 
truly glad I am not still in my twenties - you can keep this world I'm 
seeing in my sunset years - S.American so-called democracies, 
Afghanistan, the 'Two Chinas', N. Korea, Iraq, Iran, Islam, Darfur, 
Ethiopia, Zimbabwe, pollution, environment heating up, glaciers rapidly 
disappearing, current dependency upon oil, potential nuclear 
proliferation....; it goes on and on.....

Above sounds like an old guy being pessimistic ? Consider the following 
sobering thought.

As a pair of 'Anglos', with Sri Lankan friends we attend a Goan New 
Years Eve function in a German-Canadian Club. (How's zat for 
multi-culturism :-) ). Latter part of evening go outside for a smoke. 
Other functions going on in other rooms. Out comes an old gent, from 
another room. Tyrolean hat, lederhosen, and a member of a dancing party 
doing Oom pah pah. God he looked fit; had to be about 83. We chat, and 
he said, "You know", echoing the phrase, 'The War to End all Wars', 
"Having served as a Luftwaffe pilot in WWII, we haven't had a day of 
peace since the end of that war".

Jimmy
```

With all due respect to a man of such years... twaddle.  He's lived in 
good health and oompah-dancing shape to an age beyond most of his 
ancestors, who had more 'peaceful' times.

[WARNING... DIALECT HUMOR FOLLOWS]

'Yah... der vass my Onkel Louie, who shtepped on a landt-mine duringk der

undt my grandtmother, who died at 54 of 'topacco heart'... her mother 
passed on at 42, cholera... undt here I am, 83, forced to liff in zoch 
miseraple times... undt haff you heard dot bandt in dere?  I've been

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is Micro Focus doomed?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-21T09:21:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dlsvnl$p8t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<H4odf.26129$td.6293@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <a732d$43775468$4f9c624$23473@DIALUPUSA.NET> <7%Odf.502020$tl2.92202@pd7tw3no> <XsPdf.519$w84.72266@news20.bellglobal.com> <%6Rdf.502194$tl2.382248@pd7tw3no> <27_df.14132$kd.9637@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <4378C0E5.BC88F4EF@mts.net> <T_4ef.502687$1i.418040@pd7tw2no> <o%rff.189517$Ur3.117046@fe01.news.easynews.com> <K1Aff.534967$tl2.179102@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:K1Aff.534967$tl2.179102@pd7tw3no...

> I could continue this but see no point. However I would start an entirely 
> new thread labeled, "Is COBOL doomed". This is not M/F per se, but all the 
> vendors and J4. Watch Chuck bridle. It's largely the emphasis on Mainframe 
> or the phrase "Legacy applications", and the inability to acknowledge 
> variant operating systems in our Standards.

Everyone has the right to an opinion, Jimmy.  ;-)

Personally, it's my belief that the COBOL standard should avoid anything 
that smacks of acknowledging the characteristics of particular operating 
environments insofar as possible, precisely because of the extremely high 
risk that it would ignore another operating environment.  COBOL *seeks* to 
be as platform-independent as possible.  That which is platform-dependent is 
ipso facto nonstandard.

That does not mean, however, that a clever programmer is prohibited from 
programmatically exploring the implementor-defined (or undefined) results of 
certain constructs; having knowledge of the characteristics of those 
operating environments deduce from those results what the run-time operating 
environment actually is; and take action depending on that deduction.

I really don't know whether COBOL is doomed or not, but I can say that the 
more portable we can make it the better the chances of its survival.  That 
is one of the main reasons I've been working so hard with the folks drafting 
the revision to the IEEE 754 floating-point standard, as I believe producing 
arithmetic results that are *the same* regardless of the operating 
environment is a major improvement to the core language and to the core 
language's portability.   And in general, I think my contributions to the 
current COBOL standard and the draft for the next one have been in the "core 
language" arena rather than in the new-syntax area.

Whether all of this is too little, too late or not I don't know.  I'm 61 
years old now, it won't be all that many years before I retire (though I 
have to say I don't look forward to it!).  I do know that (wearing my Unisys 
MCP hat) we have a large, and growing, customer base, that an overwehelming 
majority of our customers' application programs are in one or another 
dialect of COBOL.  And I also believe that many of our users are striving to 
preserve the logic of their existing applications while providing more 
modern "front ends" to that logic, using COBOL for what it does best and 
using other languages and mechanisms where appropriate.

Why should a company replace a corporate asset -- their applications -- so 
long as that asset is serving the company well in its current state?

Also, I am aware of at least one very popular "commodity" business 
application that is written in COBOL, and I am aware of another that is 
written in a proprietary language unique to the implementor that owes a 
great deal to COBOL.  I am aware of 4GL's that generate COBOL, whether the 
end user is aware of it or not.   I am aware of more than one application 
essential to the financial well-being of *multiple countries* written in 
COBOL, and I am aware of at least one national banking system that is 
entirely dependent on COBOL today.  Moreover, I am *not* aware that any of 
the entities responsible for any of them have any particular desire to scrap 
their investment in the foreseeable future -- on the grounds that "COBOL is 
doomed" or otherwise.

I do not expect to retire before support terminates for the Unisys MCP 
*COBOL74* compiler, much less the COBOL85 compiler, and I do not expect to 
see Unisys customers abandoning COBOL (or Unisys) in droves just to jump on 
the latest application-design bandwagon.

Customers in the financial marketplace tend to be conservative, and COBOL is 
a conservative language in a number of ways.  By design I believe it seeks 
to *conserve* and *preserve* the user's investment in his programs, and I 
also believe that that is one of its strengths.   Whether COBOL ultimately 
goes the way of the Packard and the Alvis, or is perceived more like the 
products of Peterbilt and Mack, remains to be seen.   That a Bentley would 
not reasonably be expected to meet the needs of the average Kenworth user, 
and vice versa, does not spell doom for either marque.

       -Chuck Stevens
```
