```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>> First, the amount of resources a data-entry process consumes is
>> irrelevant. Whether GUI or character, it can never consume more than
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> supporting character based I/O.

From my lips to God's ears. Who cares if it's thousands of times more 
intensive? All computers wait at the same speed and the time spent by the 
computer waiting for the next character to be typed is magnitudes larger 
than the amount of time spent processing the character.

>
>> Further, a GUI interface can EXACTLY emulate a character-based
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> calculator' and picture.

Including displaying a web page?

> That vehicle license system I've described
> permits looking up help, and tables and directories of codes on *any*
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> software. You think adding GUI is going to improve that?

Of course it will. Employee morale will soar and customer trust will 
improve. Every time I see someone working on a character-based screen I 
think: "Poor bastard."

>
>> No, character-only displays or data entry screens are as archaic as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and never sold.

Odd, I've got 620 customers in the Western hemisphere (US, Canada, Mexico, 
Caribbean islands) that bought our COBOL software.

>
> Your opinion sounds like the person who has only a hammer for a tool:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> knives, just because you think they're 'nifty'? ;-)

It's not an "opinion;" it's a fact. Demostrable, provable, empirical. You 
can take it to the bank. You can let your sister marry it. You could build a 
religion around it! For example, you can't get much more character-based 
than accounting. Name an accounting system that is not GUI (Quicken, 
QuickBooks, Peachtree, Microsoft SBA, Great Plains, etc. are all GUI). There 
may be one or two character-based systems, but the vast majority are GUI. 
Facts are stubborn things.

I own a company that writes and sells software to a dynamic industry. You, 
evidently, work for an agency of government.

This, along with the concomitant view of the world by each, eloquently 
explains the differences in our outlook.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> Hardly. Not for nearly 26 years.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> imaginable purpose, or they're a "Poor Bastard."

Aw, shucks. There are those, who, when on the losing side of a fact-based 
dispute, attribute some bizarre concoction to the opposing party then 
complain the other party's position is absurd. The French have a word for 
this line of argumentation, but I don't know what it is.

I never said GUI was the panacea for every conceivable task. Obviously, 
governmental agencies are, according to your testimony, devoted to 
character-based systems and such systems are probably appropriate for them, 
considering the quality of the typical governmental worker (of course it has 
yet to be proven whether dummies need character-based systems or whether 
character-based systems turn otherwise normal folk into creatures suitable 
only for medical experimentation).

In passing, you said earlier:

"That vehicle license system I've described permits looking up help,
and tables and directories of codes on *any* field on *every* screen. Table
entries can be selected from the display and are inserted directly into the
field on the screen."

I am aware of the amount of code necessary to generate a pickable, scrolling 
table in a character-based system. What with file access, [Next Page], 
[Back], etc., I trust you'll admit coding such a routine is not 
insignificant.

In GUI COBOL, it is ONE FREAKING LINE of code!
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11oc0coaae48r06@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <11o7qgto8rvq20d@news.supernews.com> <oYZgf.15868$s92.11183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <11o97llmb7ke0f0@news.supernews.com> <qq4hf.16521$s92.2079@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
>> Including displaying a web page?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Our system does. When clerks are entering product data, they want to know 
competing prices so they can set their selling price accordingly. We do web 
look-ups to assist in that task. Our users love it.

And I bet "taxpayers" would love, as they pay their property taxes, to 
receive a free map of all the child molesters residing in their ZIP code 
(which you can get on the web).

Who knows all the uses a web-search capability can provide? Point is, with 
character-based designs, there is no hope. No hope at all. Sniff.

But some people don't mind "Molesters in the Midst."
```

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-29T20:13:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JD2jf.141464$yS6.71708@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <11o7qgto8rvq20d@news.supernews.com> <oYZgf.15868$s92.11183@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <11o97llmb7ke0f0@news.supernews.com> <qq4hf.16521$s92.2079@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:qq4hf.16521$s92.2079@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> they collect data from customers and taxpayers. ;-)

    I haven't been following this discussion too closely, but this last 
interchange reminded me of when I opened some accounts at a bank (which was 
between 0.5 and 1 year ago, about the same time I got my first "real" job).

    The bank was TD Canada Trust. I forget what was used to set up the 
chequing account (or maybe I just didn't see), but for the investment 
account, the clerk was using a web interface. As part of the procedure, they 
had to assess my "investment personality", and some of the questions were 
accompanied with charts and graphs (e.g. "if these were the history of the 
performances of funds, which one would you choose to invest in? Fund A, Fund 
B, Fund C or Fund D?")

    However, when it came time to apply for a creditcard, the program the 
clerk used was clearly character based, fixed font, 80 by 24, monochrome.

    I suspect that the People Who Make The Decisions don't really care 
either way (whether to use GUI, web, or character based); just as long as it 
works, and isn't too expensive.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T12:13:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1132776818.051751.298160@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<11o7qgto8rvq20d@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <11o7qgto8rvq20d@news.supernews.com>`

```
> And it is the GUI screen that is more robust in its ability to handle exceptions,

I've never had a 'green screen' do a 'blue screen' on me.

> informative error messages, pop-up enhancements such as
> graphical calendars and calculators,

Why do you think that you can't have a pop-up calendar and/or
calculator with text mode ? I have those in my systems on text mode.

> hooks to other data elements (ZIP code directories, parts listings, directories of similar
> names), interactions with other applications such as internet look-ups,

Why do you think that this has anything at all to do with GUI ?  My
text mode applications access other applications and connect to web
sites and extract data.

> and displays of graphics

OK, text mode is no good for pretty pictures.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T07:17:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> what the client wants. After all, it's their money. :-)

I think Heybub jumped in and answered it pretty well. Honestly, it's the 
first time I've seen you use the qualifier "Can be either Screen or 
GUIs" - I definitely had the impression you were staunchly Screen 
Section and used that as a selling point - you could do it cheaper. Like 
Heybub, without reiterating same points GUI approach can be just as 
effective and you can design to ignore the mouse - <ENTER> = your 
Default Button (PB-OK).

Now marginally only, Screen might be useful for a data-entry intensive 
application. Can you think of one. Your Kentucky (?) Licensing System - 
isn't that a come as you go person system.

Banking - there's a joint Clearing House here in Calgary for ALL 
Canadian banks operating in Alberta and parts of BC. Time-critical banks 
have to get paperwork to the Centre for an 18:00 hours start - must be 
transmitted back east to Toronto no later than 02:00 following morning.
The operation cheques - OK they've already got MICR coding at the bottom 
- the missing ink the dollar amount written by the customer - that they 
have to keypunch into MICR format so that the finals can go through an 
MICR reader prior to transmission to Toronto.

As of two years ago, as told to me by the former lady supervisor, 
because of plastic, direct debit - volumes are now only a THIRD of what 
they used to be. The problem OCR - Character Recognition - IBM hadn't 
got it licked 40 years ago. Yes there are some applications that use say 
OCR in preference to OMR, but on specially designed input forms blue 
boxes on white where you carefully write in the digits, ensuring say, 
the character "4" is centered in the box. But you can hardly do that 
with consumers who are writing cheques in random writing styles.

Even something as fairly straight-forward as Accounts Payable is now 
advantageously done with GUIs. Example Amoco has a particular play on a 
well site; to cover costs, they co-opt with Exxon, Mobil, Petro Canada 
or whoever. Contractually they figure out how to percentage share costs 
etc. As HeyBub said this can be covered by Dropdowns, Listboxes etc. 
Invariably as invoices come in they are looking for an AFE Number 
(Authorized for Expenditure) - probably you use the same term below the 
49th. The AFE identity can bring up an already authorized set of 
Chart-of-Account numbers applicable for G/L allocation. Obviously the 
percentage splits occur i the background, and so on......

Now I know you could do this with Screen Section emulating the GUI 
approach - I did it myself with M/F Screen Section before getting 
involved in Windows using their CBL_ routines for chars and mouse. No 
doubt you still have copies of Admouse.cbl and Logper.cbl - never 
understood what the latter name is an acronym for - but that's the one 
which lets you jiggle the prime set of colours around, on the fly.

Still more flexible with GUIs. A little bit too colourful for my liking, 
but Kellie did a neat job using API calls in N/E. Similarly, ever looked 
at Michael M's medical system. BASIC of course - again APIs I think - 
but very elegant. What else would you expect from a master house 
decorator :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T08:21:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<71_gf.3545$e43.239917@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't that a come as you go person system.

I think you are being blinded by relatively simple applications running
on a monolithic OS that gives you no choice.

I could have 20 or 30 threads going in a data gathering system.
Background updates, sensors, web data comming in, etc., etc. About 90%
of that stuff can run quite adequately on a glass terminal, and while
running a single GUI may be just fine, running 20 of them at once would
slow down any machine on the market to a crawl, for absolutely no
reason. Of the 30 threads, only one or two would typically do anything
but log errors.

Why cripple my machine by insisting that everything run as part of a
single application?.

Of course, the same people that say "the overhead of a GUI is
irrelevant" would also say "Why do such a thing in *Cobol*! You cannot
use a high level language for monitoring interupts, you have to use
*assembler*!"

Presumably because that gives you time to run a GUI.

If you look at a modern OS, instead of DOS with a GUI, then you have 30
to 40 processes going at any given time. *Most* of those are running on
simple glass terminals.  Then you have a GUI, which is really a *user*
interface, running as a distinct process to interface to a *user*.

If 90% of my application does not interface to a user, but only to other
computer programs and other systems, then why should I spend 95% of my
programming efforts into writing a user interface?

Its a bit like saying walking is no longer userful because a Boeing 747
will get you there a lot faster. For crossing the street to get smokes,
the 747 is detrimental, not faster. Saying "747's only cost a nickle"
does not make it convenient, or even usefull.

Even with simple IT applications the same is true. I could run my web
server, for example, in the GUI, and just minimize it at the bottom of
my screen while I do other things.  It makes a whole lot more sense,
however, to give it it's own glass terminal, and run it in the
background.  I don't *want* it popping up windows on my GUI whenever an
outside user makes an error, nor do I want to remove all screen IO and
have to run it blind.

Donald
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> I think you are being blinded by relatively simple applications running
> on a monolithic OS that gives you no choice.

Are you now having a go at me as an apostle of GUI-ing :-)

I'll tell you straight, life was a damned sight easier just using Screen 
Section ! Judson has already indicated he emulates certain so-called 
Windows features. (Would be a toughie I would suggest to emulate a 
Treeview). Coupled with the M/F CBL_screen or mouse routines I did the 
same. Was trying to work out how to emulate a scroll-bar prior to the 
OO/GUI-switch.

I don't dispute what you wrote - I see no general need to involve a GUI 
  if you are background processing, although the Progress Bar is a 
feature automatically available, plus that little feature in Webbing 
with the sprocket, top right, revolving to indicate something is happening.

However it really boils down to this. End users, folks younger even than 
you, not me, have been inculcated in, "Winders is the way to go". That's 
what's meant by being 'computer literate'. Like it or not, when your 
generation, you and Judson leave this mortal coil, Screen Section is 
absolutely dead. Some young kid coming up in programming would be 
foolish to even consider Screen Section if he wants to make it.

J4, perhaps reluctantly, having now just made Screen Section available, 
by 2010 could possibly add it to the obsolete list just like the old 
Communications bit.

Jimmy
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T17:47:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9k6hf.25239$gK4.576526@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<kr5hf.586223$1i.572901@pd7tw2no>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <71_gf.3545$e43.239917@news20.bellglobal.com> <kr5hf.586223$1i.572901@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Consumers, yes.  Young people?  No.  I live next door to the University
of Waterloo, one of the foremost CS schools in Canada.  The young
comp-sci students that I deal with *hate* GUI's.  Most of them pride
themselves on being command line guru's, which is even more basic than a
screen section. You will hear them state over and over and over ... "if
you want to be a power user, get out of the GUI and learn to use the
command line".

Tell me, if you want to copy all your cobol code to another disk area,
do you point and click for an hour, or do you "Xcopy *.cbl"?

That's the point, really.  The same one that Judson is making. It
depends on what you are trying to do.  GUI's absolutely suck for certain
things. How many Cobol coder's put a display statement in when they are
debugging, even when debugging GUI code?

*If* you are writing end user application code, and *if* you are writing
a consumer product, then a gui is the way to go. If you are not, then
you are in an altogether different situation. Lots of cobol code, I am
sure, has nothing but an operator's console.

Personally, I'd have scrapped the screen section and created a new
screen object that could be either, but that is an altogether different
story.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T06:16:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7Vchf.598754$tl2.284956@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9k6hf.25239$gK4.576526@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <71_gf.3545$e43.239917@news20.bellglobal.com> <kr5hf.586223$1i.572901@pd7tw2no> <9k6hf.25239$gK4.576526@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Dammit,

Typing merrily away, got half this message written, and have no idea
which key I hit, but suddenly I'm faced with new empty e-mail containing
about the last six words of my message - I've completely lost the previous.

> 
> Consumers, yes.  Young people?  No.  I live next door to the University
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well I find that very hard to believe. But as it's you and I know
perfectly well you wouldn't do a snow job on me, I have to accept it as
fact from your observations. Refreshing in a way. As McGill is to
Medicine, so Waterloo to Computing. But having been forced into 
re-typing this, a thought. Like every place else, highly likely Waterloo 
  may have given up on COBOL. So these young Turks are they into 
primarily Xenix/Unix/Linux mode coupled with Java or C ? Is that why 
they are enthused about the command line ? Well over twenty years ago, I 
had to learn sufficient Xenix command line stuff to get RM/COBOL off the 
ground for the four-station multi user system. I positively HATED their 
cryptic command lines :-

/t /-w /c ....... crap like that. Oh sure it was powerful, but why does 
life have to include that sort of cryptic challenge.

> Tell me, if you want to copy all your cobol code to another disk area,
> do you point and click for an hour, or do you "Xcopy *.cbl"?

Now I do think we are starting to compare apples to pears at this stage.
I'm thinking 'ONLY' GUIs for Dialog boxes that communicate with a user 
in a given application, not run his machine for him. Some have 
previously been mentioned: from the little use I make of it Quickbooks 
is superb with its inter-activity with the user, plus just ONE file 
containing all data, regardless of how many features you use and 
sessions finishing with "Do you want to do a backup ?'.

Now I certainly agree with your comments about Xcopy with wildcards - 
but from memory only ever used it once. But that criticism is not a 
justifiable reason to dismiss GUIs. It most certainly is a justifiable 
criticism of MicroSoft for trying to get us to work in a totally Windows 
environment. Yes you can get at your Xcopy through the 'Run" box. But a 
quite simple solution - depending where you want it to sit in the 
Windows features, "Line Commands" which to us are the old DOS line 
commands. Perhaps make a dropdown list available within Windows, plus 
access to the help syntax associated with command line. Yes it's there 
in the background if we dig, like I found reference to the old DIR :-

dir \mydirectory\*.cbl  /w /O:n > appfiles.txt

And then when I now read you further down, that's the kind of point you 
are making.

> That's the point, really.  The same one that Judson is making. It
> depends on what you are trying to do.  GUI's absolutely suck for certain
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> story.

Sure agree with the last bit. Put on your thinking hat - I have no 
details but that's what I used to refer to as Acu's dual screen system. 
  I don't know details but it could be used in traditional DOS text mode 
or would produce Windows-oriented GUIs. How effective it was I don't 
know. From their site, they dropped it and went for an outside product 
for GUI-ing. Now of course if theirs, or your vision, which is directly 
on the ball, gelled with OO then we are home and dry.

So :-

- GUIs we generate for our customers
- a new Windows Command line feature - for the housekeeping background
	stuff.

Naturally the tecchie user could independently use the 'new Windows line 
command'. But caution some of them get too tecchie. My guy, firstly he 
used DOS macros to take him through my cycle of screen prompts, having 
worked out what the programs were asking him to do in sequence. But 
there again, same guy, took me twenty years to find out what was going 
wrong. He's operating some 7 hours north, way out in the boonies 
perhaps, from a well adapted van; he's not sitting on an orange box. 
Comfortable padded chair like the one I'm using while typing this, desk, 
heating, lights, a desktop firmly bolted down for traveling and his 
ultrasonic instruments sat along side.

Always complaining he was getting index crashes on ISAMs - status 98 on 
RM/COBOL. Bit clumsy, but RM gave you the source and copying their 
templates  you had to compile for a SPECIFIC file. So lose 6 files meant 
copy/paste and change for at least 6 compiles followed by 6 rebuilds, 
and records could get lost in the conversion. Long ago forgotten the 
pattern but I automated this function for him through a DOS batch file; 
he supplied the copyfile name for the file (FD) and the external name, 
(Oil Company/Plant).

I could never get a handle on why he was getting so many errors and 
REGULARLY - I wasn't getting ANY back here in development mode.

A terrible perfectionist, and became cynical based on experience. Latter 
years he used to grumble consistently about the poor quality of people 
he employed. No pride in job, lacking curiosity etc., etc.... Much of 
course which is true. He was hiring/firing more times than I was having 
hot dinners. Why is it everybody else is wrong ?

Back to that van. All the electricals - powered by a gas generator, put 
outside the rear door of the van. He's sat in the van doing the input 
while his helpers, mostly one, is/are  on the other end of a cable with 
an ultrasonic probe held against a vessel or pipe to get a decimalized 
inch reading of thicknesses. Then he complained, that so often THEY 
forgot to put sufficient gas into that small generator. BOOM ! Lights 
out, computer shuts down. THEY ? He had been at the game for over twenty 
years and this was consistently happening - Hello. As the owner and 
person most deeply involved, shouldn't gassing up the generator be a 
*major* consideration before you start your work day regardless of who 
is designated to fill the generator.

Even the very cleverest users can really screw it up.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T07:48:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> I think Heybub jumped in and answered it pretty well.

I don't! He's as closed minded as you think I am. :-)

> Honestly, it's the first time I've seen you use the qualifier "Can be 
> either Screen or GUIs" - I definitely had the impression you were 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> your Default Button (PB-OK).

That's just your knee-jerk reaction, Jimmy. :-)  No, you can search through 
my messages dilligently, and you will not find anywhere I ever said GUI was 
bad or to be avoided at all costs. GUI is wonderful for lots of things. What 
I have been saying all along hasn't changed: to use the best tool for the 
job, and that I believe going "all GUI, no character, ever" is throwing the 
baby out with the wash, because some applications are actually better (and 
simpler, and easier, and cheaper, and more reliably) done in character 
interface.

> Now marginally only, Screen might be useful for a data-entry intensive 
> application. Can you think of one. Your Kentucky (?) Licensing System - 
> isn't that a come as you go person system.

Yes, the Jefferson County, Alabama Vehicle License System is a good example. 
There would simply be no benefit to making that system GUI. Or to hundreds 
and thousands of similar systems in use by companies and government 
agencies. There are probably millions of people doing this kind of data 
entry. When GUI is needed to meet some user need or want, then use GUI. But 
when it isn't needed, use character.

> <examples snipped>

Jimmy, I really believe in simplicity. Never make your job or your 
application any more complicated than necessary. But always make it as 
complicated as necessary to do the best job for the user. If that means GUI, 
then use GUI. But if you don't (and won't) benefit measurably from using GUI 
for a particular application, then go the simpler, cheaper, more robust 
character based route. If the user (or the market) wants GUI, then by all 
means give it to them, it's their money. But when they let us choose, then 
we should choose what will best serve the user, and that includes cost, 
time, resources and reliability.

To be honest, It amazes me that I get such heated objections for taking this 
position, which I believe is balanced and reasonable. I can only think that 
people become so enamoured with the nifty-gee-whiz things they *can* do in 
GUI, they start thinking they *should* to do them, even if there is no need, 
want or benefit. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T23:15:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
 

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
>
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
Some well made and fair points, Judson. It made me re-evaluate my position.

I use GUI exclusively. (I am experimenting with voice input, which I believe 
will be the "next thing". It is kinda fun because it is still a "bleeding 
edge" technology, but it gets better each year.) Touch screens are also very 
useful and you can't really do them UNLESS it is GUI. The touch co-ordinates 
are returned through the mouse driver so it is just like a left click.

You can use a numeric keypad or full text into a GUI text box just as easily 
as you can into a green screen, so when I say "GUI" I don't necessarily mean 
point and click. (Although that is also an option, which you don't have if 
it is NOT a GUI screen.)

I would not consider using SCREEN SECTION. (But that's just me, and I don't 
think anyone who does so is "wrong". I DID use it in the 1980s, but you 
grow... :-))

Your post makes me think that just because cars were invented, horses didn't 
stop working. But I can't clearly remember the last time I rode a horse...

(I was in my teens... and the bloody thing preferred to graze the fresh 
ensilage, rather than give me a ride. I remember the owners telling me to 
"kick it in the guts", but I couldn't bring myself to do that. I knew I 
wouldn't do that, and the horse kinew I wouldn't do that, and I knew that 
the horse knew that I wouldn't do that, so I just sat there, enjoying the 
sunshine while the horse grazed and its owners kept screaming abuse at it 
and inciting me to extreme violence from afar. Not much of a horseride 
really. The horse kind of nuzzled me when I finally got off, so I guess it 
was trying to say: "Thanks" (or maybe it was just glad to be rid of my 
weight... :-))

On balance, I think SCREEN SECTION and text screens don't do it for me. 
However, I use OO and quick build tools like PowerCOBOL, VB, Java swing and 
metal classes, so interfacing to a GUI is absolutely no problem. I like GUI 
components for things like grids, numeric input, and list and treeviews 
also, and I guess the main advantage of a GUI (as I see it) is that I can 
drop components on it that can save me weeks of programming. My GUI screen 
can be a container for things like data access components, that are 
invisible to the user. (The screen has an icon on it that is not visible, 
but it means that all the Methods and Properties that are availabe through 
that component, are now "contained" by the screen and so they are inherited 
down to anything that uses that screen. In fact, I can and do use my GUI 
screens to "Package" and encapsulate processing via components, that is 
pertinent to that screen. I can't do that with SCREEN SECTION.

It is all about what we are comfortable with.

BTW, I don't think you need to be defensive about what you do. It works. 
Good.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T07:35:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11obcthqvjrfr45@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net...
[snip]
>  Touch screens are also very
> useful and you can't really do them UNLESS it is GUI. The touch
co-ordinates
> are returned through the mouse driver so it is just like a left click.

I suppose that depends upon how one defines
'touch screen'.

ATMs use 'touch' with a 'screen' and these are
character-based with no mouse. The coordinate
of a button next to the screen is used.

Automated payment processing at gasoline (petrol)
stations use 'touch' with a 'screen' of characters
overlaying buttons. The coordinate is determined
by scanning for button presses. Again, no mouse.

On the other hand, many fast-food places use
graphical depictions of food items where the
order is entered using 'touch screen', but no
mouse (for health safety reasons <g>).

Thus, if one uses 'screen' as 'a surface on which
images are formed' and uses 'touch' to imply
contact with that surface, then one may see a lot
of character-based uses for 'touch screen'.
```

- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net> <11obcthqvjrfr45@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'touch screen'.

No implication at all - the reference was to YOU-TOUCH-THE-SCREEN. You 
are splitting hairs.
> 
> ATMs use 'touch' with a 'screen' and these are
> character-based with no mouse. The coordinate
> of a button next to the screen is used.

Customized equipment - ATMs have a screen with a simple display of 
options. As an adjunct you are using Pushbuttons either side of the 
screen. You can run your little pinkies all up and down the ATM screen 
and it wont do a thing for you.

> Automated payment processing at gasoline (petrol)
> stations use 'touch' with a 'screen' of characters
> overlaying buttons. The coordinate is determined
> by scanning for button presses. Again, no mouse.

Same as ATMs but a much smaller 'window' for the screen.

> On the other hand, many fast-food places use
> graphical depictions of food items where the
> order is entered using 'touch screen', but no
> mouse (for health safety reasons <g>).
> 
Still not 'touch screen' - a dedicated keyboard. Haven't been in a 
McDonalds in decades, but perhaps they fit. I know our own Tim Hortons 
(coffee and doughnut outlets - now part of Wendys), have a dedicated 
keyboard, (a two-dimensional, something like 12 x 12); I think that 
applies to many of the Pizza franchises.

> Thus, if one uses 'screen' as 'a surface on which
> images are formed' and uses 'touch' to imply
> contact with that surface, then one may see a lot
> of character-based uses for 'touch screen'.

This approach doesn't have much impact on major retail chains, 
(department stores/fashion chains/supermarkets) where they have 
dedicated/customized POS systems scanning SKUs from UPCs.

Now for the small guys, example Gardening with some 6 outlets in a city, 
and they want to get into POS. Smaller software houses will produce POS 
applications with different options. The one I like locally is a PC with 
a TOUCH SCREEN. The screen is taken up with a large display of a number 
pad. The check-out girl TOUCHES the individual numbers on the screen, 
either keying in their own local SKU or querying something without a 
price ticket.

Net Express user, Bob Hennessey down in the States, works for a small 
software house -  POS applications. Apparently adapting to touch screen 
programming is no big deal.

As to Doc's comments - true I haven't seen much pen stuff, but my family 
doctor always has a pen in his hand, and it's a light pen, zipping away 
through his software, extremely sophisticated, 'poking' the screen with 
the light pen. It is only at the end of the session, where if necessary 
he will pick up one of those things with some liquid inside, and then 
perhaps write you a prescription. (The doctor truly believes in a 
'paperless society' - all paperwork is scanned and immediately available 
through a touch with his light pen).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T13:59:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WWohf.19809$s92.4608@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net> <11obcthqvjrfr45@corp.supernews.com> <8vnhf.601960$1i.251985@pd7tw2no>`

```
There is a Wal-Mart near here that is apparently testing a self checkout 
system. At least, I hope they're just testing it. Boy, would I really like 
to have a word with whoever designed that thing! It is clear that the person 
who designed it never actually used it in a real situation. It is the 
dumbest, most counterintuitive system of the kind I have ever seen. Last 
time I went through one, every cashier had a line of several people, but 
*nobody* was going through the auto checkouts. As soon as one of the 
managers saw me walking toward one, she started toward me. Obviously, she 
knew from experience that nobody went through that thing unassisted. I won't 
try to describe all the problems, but when you register an item, the system 
expects you to place the item in a bag on a scale, where it can be weighed. 
You must place the item in that area *precisely* when it expects it, not too 
soon. Apparently in development they must have timed some person afflicted 
with severe arthritis or something, because I was constantly putting the 
item in the bag too soon, unable to believe it wanted me to wait *that* 
long. I registered one tiny item, waited, then put it in the bag. Evidently, 
the item was too light to register on the scale, so the system just sat 
there, telling me to put the item in the bag. You can press a 'skip bagging' 
key, but only twice during checkout. Note that if there are two glitches of 
any kind, you're out - the system waits for a manager. All the while, this 
poor manager stood there with a grim expression on her face, having to enter 
an override code after almost every item I entered caused a problem. I 
wanted to rant and rave, but I felt too sorry for the poor lady - it wasn't 
her fault. I contented myself with gnashing my teeth, describing the design 
engineer's obvious mental facilities, and ripping out a bit more of what 
little hair I have left. It wasn't the delay that bothered me so much, it 
was the sheer, unbelievable stupidity of the design. Justice would be to 
force 100 criminals to go through that thing, then pen them up with the 
person who designed it. This thing must have been designed by the CEO or 
president's fair haired kid or something, because Wal-Mart is a savvy 
competitor, and I can't imagine them inflicting this thing on their 
customers for long.

In contrast, the grocery store chain Winn-Dixie (there's a recent movie 
about a dog in a Winn-Dixie store) has auto checkout stations that work much 
better, and lots of customers use them. Ironically, Winn-Dixie filed for 
Chapter 11 bankruptcy earlier this year (For those not familiar with US 
bankruptcy law - Chapter 11 means you want to restructure, get debt relief, 
and make a comeback - Chapter 13 means you want to liquidate & shut the 
doors).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-25T04:43:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CDwhf.612665$tl2.112695@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<WWohf.19809$s92.4608@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net> <11obcthqvjrfr45@corp.supernews.com> <8vnhf.601960$1i.251985@pd7tw2no> <WWohf.19809$s92.4608@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> There is a Wal-Mart near here that is apparently testing a self checkout 
> system. At least, I hope they're just testing it. Boy, would I really like 
> to have a word with whoever designed that thing! It is clear that the person 

Well I quickly glanced it Judson, but take your point. My concerns for 
the future - younger people, and I'm generalizing, wont have the savvy 
on systems. Oh yes, they are absolute whiz kids in programming. My 
background shuffling paper in the RAF and two years of civvy accounting. 
The eventual job was automate it with a compooter.

Even when we are real up close, sometimes me miss the obvious. To me my 
own personal tragic case. A hard-working Chinese, probably ex Hong Kong, 
started up a family business in groceries here, possibly early 'fifties. 
Family members and of course fellow Chinese employees in the main. They 
worked hard and grew to some six outlets in Calgary. Meanwhile #1 Son, 
and then #2 Son, through to #3, grew up, went to university here and 
learned how to *really* run a business.

Obviously competition from Safeway, Co-Op, Local buyers group IGA, 
Superstore from Montreal etc. As you can imagine, although 
entrepreneurial, Dad was cautious with his money. #'s 1 through 3 were 
saying, "We have to get bigger, so that we can negotiate more 
competitive purchase prices". They duly did; a huge box store opens very 
close to me at an intersection McLeod Trail/Canyon Meadows. (Thinking 
back Richard, about the size of one of the aircraft hangars at 
Northolt). New outlet, certainly from first day, and tapered off over 
the first week, long lines of customers wanting to get in, sample and buy.

Interesting, good range of merchandise (food) but not any excitement 
aroused. Nice looking veggies so pick some up, put them in a plastic bag 
and eventually head for the check-out. You get there - "Oh you have to 
go back to the Veggie Section to weight them". "Bugger !", say this 
customer, others slightly more polite. Then you return to the 
check-pout. Now I know generally speaking Chinese people are small in 
stature. But the check-outs had been designed for mini-mini-Chinese. You 
were literally on your knees putting stuff onto the check-out desk.

Don't think prices were particularly competitive, no 'interesting' 
merchandise, remember to weigh those bloody veggies and get down on your 
knees to go through the check-outs.  Lucky if we went there once in two 
months, although only about some five minutes from home by car. Last 
time we went, big sheets of plain plastic hanging over large areas of 
the store - closed off area where there was no merchandise. Roughly a 
year after it opened, goes belly-up, bankrupt and Dad loses all his 
money. Really sad. Now the same site is occupied by a 3 or 4 movie 
complex, fitness club, offshoot of an engineering firm, Subway 
sandwiches, etc.

Oh I can relate to your self-help incident. I've already said I hate 
traveling. Eileen went to UK about two years ago. Air Canada had 
instituted a new compooterised system at Calgary airport. Self serve to 
save you the hassle of going through the booking-in - in theory - just 
like you a 'helper' was around to help the 'occasional' customer. 
Occasional = EVERY customer at that time. To be honest it worked real 
quick when it was the HELPER who did the keying in.

So Eileen makes it to the Departure Lounge. I make it for home and my 
doggies, and the two cats, thinking, "I love her. But sooner her than me".

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:8vnhf.601960$1i.251985@pd7tw2no...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> are splitting hairs.

Consider,
    class TouchScreen inherits from Base

    class AtmScreen inherits from TouchScreen
    class GasPumpScreen inherits from TouchScreen
    class PosScreen inherits from TouchScreen

If one defines 'touch screen' as an abstraction,
other uses may become more apparent.

If one defines 'touch screen' as
"YOU-TOUCH-THE-SCREEN" and "[t]he touch
co-ordinates are returned through the mouse driver",
there aren't as many uses.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-25T06:27:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59yhf.605890$oW2.314521@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<11ocvelpufr8e72@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net> <11obcthqvjrfr45@corp.supernews.com> <8vnhf.601960$1i.251985@pd7tw2no> <11ocvelpufr8e72@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:8vnhf.601960$1i.251985@pd7tw2no...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well that's a little bit like trying to fit a light pen into the same 
class as a morse-code tapper :-). You can of course debate until the cow 
jumps over the moon what a class should or should not include.

Possibly the highest level of the hierarchy, as we are talking input 
should perhaps be "Input Class", obviously typing away 'el fresco' I 
haven't given it deep thought, perhaps the next layer is something like :-

paper-tape, punch cards, keyboard, OCR, OMR, Screens, light-pens, 
mag-tapes, diskettes, CDs etc.

With me so far. Bit of a toss-up - could use say 'Keyboard' as the super 
  or perhaps 'Screens' as the super to embrace ATMs, Gas-Tellers, 
Touch-Screens.

I think you are bang on the button about getting the event for the 
'screen touch' giving you the x, y co-ordinates. (That event of course 
applies to both finger-touching or light-pen touching). Could be quite a 
fascinating topic, if somebody had the time to while away.

It's worth remembering, probably a fair sized team with vendors so they 
may have conceivably spent a year or two just figuring out logical 
hierarchy structures, to let them wring out every ounce of oomph from 
the various levels of supers.

Recall somewhere I wrote - I only use two levels. Requires quite a bit 
of thought and time to think in greater depth, by which time your 
application could be obsolescent before it hits the market. Now given 
the source for what somebody has already done - a second party can look 
at the thing, as it were, from a distance and see immediate enhancements 
that can be made.

That's actually the situation with me using Will Price's original 
sources for OO File Classes, which he would be likely to agree with. In 
my case the 'immediate enhancements' was not true.   Part of the problem 
was getting a 'comfort zone' with OO. While I have come a long way since 
I started, I still consider myself to have 'Learner plates' on my vehicle.

As a result of something I must have posted when first joining c.l.c. in 
'99 (?), Ed Arranga was lurking in the background and contacted me. 
Subsequently we spent about half an hour on the phone, (on his dime) 
chatting. His background was Pascal before tackling COBOL. Using Pscal, 
as he put it, he had an "Epiphany", rather like Saul (St. Paul) on the 
road to Tarsus. He carried this idea over to COBOL. Well, in turn the 
Arranga/Coyle book, but particularly the source application at the back, 
was my 'Epiphany'.

I remember at the time saying something like, "I'll never get the hang 
of it". Primarily I was referring to the GUIs. He assured me I would. I 
doubted it; but slowly, even with the GUIs it has fallen into place - 
tackling them with an all-purpose template. Even now, I'm sure no expert 
at GUIs, and know absolutely nothing about Webbing.

I took Will's idea - I looked, adapted, and refined. Next who comes 
along can do a much more proficient job than I did. They might be 
absolutely adamant that you only check for the first '0' of file-status :-)

PS: Having referenced it once today I had Adele Goldberg's 
'Smalltalk-80' on my desk while writing this. Checked to see if there 
was anything comparative to what we are discussing. No initial luck, 
then I tried the word 'input' from the index and came up with a 
reference to the following class structure :-

--------------------------------
-- Behavior
  	Class Description
	Class MetaClass

Point
Rectangle
BitBit
	CharacterScanner
	Pen

DisplayObject
	DisplayMedium
		Form
			Cursor
			DisplayScreen

	InfiniteForm
	OpaqueForm
	Path
		Arc
			Circle
		Curve
		Line
		LineFit
		Spline

--------------------------------

Jimmy
```

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:59yhf.605890$oW2.314521@pd7tw1no...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> class as a morse-code tapper :-).

H'm, discrete binary event in local device versus binary
stream to remote device. I don't see the connection!

> Possibly the highest level of the hierarchy, as we are talking input
> should perhaps be "Input Class", obviously typing away 'el fresco' I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mag-tapes, diskettes, CDs etc.

I think you're mixing device classes. COBOL has
a nice abstraction for classifying devices by their
ability to store data: sequential stoage medium
and mass storage medium.

Sequential storage allows either input only or output
only. Mass allows both input and output and adds
positioning. Some of the devices you listed are not
storage media, or at least not in the same class.
Input from magnetic stripe, bar code readers, and
proximity detectors are discrete events with a buffer
of chaacters. Output to a display has the notion of
buffer with positioning. Light pens, push buttons, and
other 'touch' devices trigger discrete events with no
buffer of characters.

I don't see how those that may be used for input can
be lumped into 'Input Class', since some may also be
used for output.

> With me so far. Bit of a toss-up - could use say 'Keyboard' as the super
>   or perhaps 'Screens' as the super to embrace ATMs, Gas-Tellers,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> fascinating topic, if somebody had the time to while away.
```

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3uqmlhF10l938U1@individual.net...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or how one defines "really"...

My dictionary provides only two adverbial uses for
'really': 'in reality; actually' and 'genuinely or truly'.

> I am aware of the arguments you make below and was aware when I posted.
> They're just not important to me.

Which one might have inferred by your stating
'I use GUI exclusively'; but this does not change
the fact that your generalization, 'you can't really
do them UNLESS it is GUI' is arguably
incorrect. And it does not matter whether 'them'
refers only to 'touch screens' or to both 'voice
input' and 'touch screens'.

>
> > ATMs use 'touch' with a 'screen' and these are
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes.  And your point is?

One can do 'touch screens' without GUI.

> None of the above can do any of the things I noted are possible with GUI
> screens (and which you cut from your response).

Of the items you mentioned: voice input, touch
screens, numeric keypad, full text, and point and
click, all are possible without GUI screens.

For the first two, one would not do voice input
(entry of PIN) in a public area for security reasons,
instead the keypad is used and entry of full text is
not required for the applications.

Note that voice input is used, sometimes, over the
telephone and a telephone is not a GUI. In those
cases, I still prefer the keypad.

> I guess our interests are in different places.

I think our experiences are different. I have twenty
years experience collecting data at its source, using
a variety of input devices attached to a terminal with
a character-based display. So, perhaps, 'different
places', at its source versus deferred until it can be
entered on a PC with a GUI, is not so far off. But,
the latter *choice* can not be used to deny
alternatives without evoking some disagreement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-11-24T10:07:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1132855623.125499.196280@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net>`

```
> (I am experimenting with voice input, which I believe
> will be the "next thing".

It has been 'the next thing' for nearly 20 years.  OS/2 came with voice
recognition 10 years ago. In the early 80s meat works here in NZ used
voice input because it was inappropriate to use a keyboard.

> Touch screens are also very
> useful and you can't really do them UNLESS it is GUI. The touch co-ordinates
> are returned through the mouse driver so it is just like a left click.

You can use a mouse (and thus a touch screen) effectively with text
mode or even with screen section. With MF you get an ADIS crt status of
27 and pick up the co-ordinates. Just put a text boxes on the screen
and put these in a table for checking x and y to see what was
'pressed', or change focus to a field if they click that, I even did
ribbon menus, it isn't hard. The HP 150 of 1983/4 was an early touch
screen that was text only.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-26T19:44:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U98if.10315$KP1.3589@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132391727.534963.214830@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <2112a$437f4c80$4f9c6a8$13616@DIALUPUSA.NET> <SNPff.538553$1i.244696@pd7tw2no> <Htqgf.449$F%3.49@trnddc05> <Sjsgf.565096$1i.549327@pd7tw2no> <lua6o1dj9adb5bfj7frqhep545vgdrvr0h@4ax.com> <oDJgf.576604$tl2.335642@pd7tw3no> <e1Lgf.2251$e43.189023@news20.bellglobal.com> <G%Mgf.570118$oW2.515137@pd7tw1no> <%7Rgf.15098$i7.7503@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <yIUgf.574976$oW2.569743@pd7tw1no> <%x_gf.15881$s92.13059@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <3uli66F11il6aU1@individual.net> <1132855623.125499.196280@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <3uqnsaF12m5otU1@individual.net> <3usfa8F12unleU1@individual.net>`

```
Back in the 80's I was asked to look into voice recognition for the Dental 
Health section of the local Health Department. They wanted the dentists to 
speak the results of dental exams (a series of number and letter codes) into 
a PC based dental statistics system I had written for them. This would have 
freed up the dental technicians who were keying the data in as the dentist 
worked, permitting them to serve more patients with the same staff. We 
looked at several PC based systems up to about $1,200, but they simply were 
not up to the task. They were *very* picky. You could 'train' the software 
to recognize one person's voice, which took from 30-90 minutes, and it would 
work fairly well for that one person. But the next day, or after lunch, or 
even after a glass of water, it wouldn't work correctly. And we only needed 
it to recognize about 8-10 letters, the numeric digits 0-9, and about five 
command words.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol books & experiences

_(thread depth: 10)_

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:U98if.10315$KP1.3589@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
>
> Back in the 80's I was asked to look into voice recognition for the Dental 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> numeric digits 0-9, and about five command words.

I saw a demo in Las Vesgas a few years back of a VR system from a company 
called Phoenix Systems. It didn't need training, and could even cope with 
different voices and, to some extent, accents. (It was running on a Sun 
system and took all available resources...  I haven't heard of them since so 
I don't know what happened. Dragon is pretty good (once you've trained it). 
I think if you evaluated a modern system, Judson, you would be agreeably 
surprised at the progress made in the last 20 years. They are still not 
perfect, but each release is better than the last and, as processor power 
increases, these CPU intensive tasks become more viable.  I can see the 
general public going for ""Close the pod bay door Hal" or "Prepare the 
lounge for seduction, please, Hal. (Lights dim, appropriate music starts, 
room becomes a few degrees warmer... etc.)... If we've seen it in the 
movies, we want it. Life emulates art. :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> it in the movies, we want it. Life emulates art. :-)


Oh, I agree Pete, the technology today is very much improved. Sorry if I 
gave the impression that I was panning voice recognition; only commenting on 
the state of the art on PCs in the 80's. :-)

My car has OnStar, which uses voice recognition to control the cell phone. 
In some ways it's pretty impressive. In recognizing the commands and spoken 
phone number digits, it works very well. But it works poorly on recognizing 
verbal phone number tags you create yourself. My first OnStar vehicle was a 
$53,000 2003 Cadillac deVille, and when I took it to the dealer for 
scheduled maintenance, I complained about that problem with the voice 
recognition. They checked it out and said it was working normally. This was 
disappointing in a vehicle of that price, because it was virtually unable to 
distinguish between phrases like "Christy cell" and "Christy home."
```

```
>>> When I said "next thing" I was talking in terms of public awareness and
>>> cyber "fashion", not the technology itself.

> If we've seen it in the movies, we want it. Life emulates art. :-)
> I can see the general public going for ""Close the pod bay door Hal"

2001 was released in 1968, that is nearly 40 years since it has had
'public awareness'. VR has been available cheaply for at least a decade
from IBM, Dragon, and several others. I tried it, others tried it, even
Microsoft talked it up. A few years ago there was a free version on a
magazine CD.  It failed to catch on. Not becase (IMHO) it wasn't good
enough, but because people don't think and talk at the same time,
wheras they do think and type.
```

```
Richard wrote:
>>>>When I said "next thing" I was talking in terms of public awareness and
>>>>cyber "fashion", not the technology itself.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
"but because people don't think and talk as the same time.......". My 
wife does, which is an easy way for us to get into a real good argument.

"Explain computers to me..."

"See this Icon here on the screen.... The one there top-left...."

"Where ?"

"That one...". (Me touching the screen Rick, not some ancillary button :-) )

"Yes. But I want to do some word processing or I want to get into the Web".

"I'm taking you there, if only you will follow me patiently, step by 
step before your next question ".

"But.......", she replies

My voice becomes louder, my diction clearer to stress words, and 
suitably laced with colourful expletives.

Sorry, even if she could get a freebie course on Computers 101, with a 
throw-in of free frequent flyer points - it still wouldn't do her any good.

PS: Still love her though.

Jimmy
```

```
 
Jimmy, I would love to respond to this, but I won't... :-)

(I will gently suggest, that, just as teaching loved ones to drive is not 
recommended, the same may be true of other endeavours. Teaching is an 
acquired skill; I did an adult teaching course at the Bay Of Plenty 
Polytechnic last year, partly because I wanted to refresh my skills (I 
started life as a teacher and became a programmer; in fact I was just out of 
Training College when I first started programming), and partly because I 
thought it would be fun. It was; and worth every penny of the significant 
fee I paid to do it. It was hard, but interesting work. I learned (among 
many other things) that the needs of adults when being taught are 
significantly different from the needs of children, and the way that each of 
us learns is a very individual process. Yet I have spent years of my life 
training people with considerable success. I now realise, I could have done 
it better. Even without the framed "Certificate in Adult Teaching" on my 
wall, I consider that course was more valuable than many I have done, 
including my formal academic studies. Highly recommended.)

Pete.
```

<a id="t-ca5560c96a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL-two-level table — 13 this month / 14 total

### COBOL-two-level table

- **From:** nc1200@sbcglobal.net
- **Date:** 2005-11-22T17:20:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello, new to this group. I am taking COBOL and the topic is
multidimensional tables. We are Loading a two-level table from a input
file. There are no indexes. We are required to use subscripts. Does
anyone have an example on how to declare the table in the data division
and working storage section. I think I have that right. Next, I need to
see an example on how to load a two-level table and how to access it. I
have not problems with a inbedded table.  A simple example would be
appreciated. Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL-two-level table

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-22T21:46:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
nc1200@sbcglobal.net wrote:
> Hello, new to this group. I am taking COBOL and the topic is
> multidimensional tables. We are Loading a two-level table from a input
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple example would be appreciated. Thank you

01 MY-TABLE.
  02  FILLER OCCURS 3.
    05  MY-TABLE-1 OCCURS 4  PIC 9.

....

PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I > 3
  PERFORM VARYING J FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL J > 4
     MOVE I TO MY-TABLE-1(I J)
     END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM.

results in a memory layout of

111122223333

whereas (changing I to J in the MOVE statement) results in

123412341234

I've never had much reason to use multi-dimensional tables in the real 
world. It's a necessary tool to have in your kit, though. At least 
understanding how they work may be useful.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL-two-level table

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T06:36:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t5Ugf.577118$1i.302937@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> nc1200@sbcglobal.net wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Please, please, please before jumping in again.

A respected heart surgeon is teaching qualified doctors as student 
surgeons. He takes them through the theory, implications etc., and they 
through the glass in an operating room watch him perform real surgeries.

Close to qualification he now wants to get them into the real world. 
Perform their own heart operations. Naturally he's stood away from the 
table to give his student leeway, but he's there should something go 
terribly wrong. Student does quite well, but makes a bit of a mess of 
the final suturing - so the surgeon takes over to refine it a bit.

Now ask yourself, is the surgeon doing the student or patient any good, 
by jumping in, picking up the cutters, knife or whatever and doing it 
showing the student how it should be done. He qualifies the student as 
'passed'. Perhaps the former student surgeon on his third op makes a 
goof - one very dead patient.

Jimmy

> 01 MY-TABLE.
>   02  FILLER OCCURS 3.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> understanding how they work may be useful.
```

```
There was an old, bad joke about something similar... the punch-line being 
'So the doctor says to the waiter 'I'm sorry, this isn't my table.'!'

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL-two-level table

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T13:12:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dm1ps9$f2e$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com>`

```
In article <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com>,
HeyBub <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I've never had much reason to use multi-dimensional tables in the real 
>world.

On my present client's site they're used as a backbone to the processing 
and this might provide a kind of readily-grasped demonstration.  For 
reporting hours worked by a given employee... the simplest sort of record 
would show that employee-number XA1 worked regular hours (type 100) on 
Monday through Friday.  Given an input layout of:

01  HRS-WKD-REC.
    05  EMPNO  PIC X(03).
    05  HRSTYP PIC X(03).
    05  DAYWKD OCCURS 14 PIC 99V99.

... then the actual data-record (given DAYWKD(1) = Sunday and DAYWKD(7) = 
Saturday) might look something like:

XA11000000080008000800080008000000

... or a reasonable facsimile thereof.  Now assume a similar week with a 
paid holiday (Hours TYPe 200) on Tuesday; to accurately reflect the 
employee's time one would need two records:

XA11000000080000000800080008000000
XA12000000000008000000000000000000

... showing 0 Regular Hours on Tuesday and 8 Paid Holiday hours on the 
same Tuesday.  When the working-storage record is coded to reflect the 
possibility of multiple records of this sort it might be:

01  HRS-WKD-REC OCCURS 100.
    05  EMPNO  PIC X(03).
    05  HRSTYP PIC X(03).
    05  DAYWKD OCCURS 14 PIC 99V99.

... and after loading with this employee's data the empty Tuesday in 
Regular Hours record would be DAYWKD(1,3) while the Paid Holiday hours 
would be found in DAYWKD(2,3).

>It's a necessary tool to have in your kit, though. At least 
>understanding how they work may be useful.

It ain't graphically interfaced, no... it's just the guts of a system that 
close to 70,000 people rely on to get paid every other week.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL-THREE-level table

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T08:47:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xf%gf.15900$s92.14365@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com> <dm1ps9$f2e$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
Curious, I did a brief search for times I had used a 3 dimensional table 
(don't think I ever used more than 3), and came up with the following. I 
think the background for this program is interesting.

Back in the 80's I wrote a system called OBAR to collect obstetrical 
history, problems and treatment information from OB/GYN doctors across the 
county, then make that information available to any other OB/GYN in the 
county to whom the mother might go for a problem or delivery. It was an easy 
system to write, but boring, no challenge. As part of OBAR, they wanted a 
separate but related Ultrasound component that collected a number of 
measurements from the ultrasound exams, and for each exam print a data sheet 
with graphic charts plotting fetal growth against norms using several 
different formulas for calculating fetal growth. Everything but printing the 
graphical charts was a piece of cake. But writing a COBOL program (the user 
didn't have any other language programmers and wanted to be able to maintain 
the program) on an EBCDIC mainframe to print a graphics chart on an 8-bit 
graphics laser printer connected over a 7-bit ASCII communication line 
running Burroughs/Unisys Poll/Select protocol was a seriously non-trivial 
task. My client was convinced it was impossible without inordinate costs.

I started by writing a basic graphics library in COBOL, bit plot, line draw, 
character draw, the works. For simplicity, I used 4-bit digits as 'bits', 
which the Unisys A Series could easily access in COBOL. It was really 
strange writing Bressenham's line drawing algorithm in COBOL. Anyway, that 
was lots of fun and permitted creating a graphic chart in the COBOL program.

But the really fun part was printing the chart on the laser printer. I wound 
up constructing the printer graphics command sequences in memory, breaking 
them down into 6-bit chunks, translating those into EBCDIC characters which, 
when translated to ASCII by the commuincations hardware, would result in 
printable ASCII characters which, when bit shifted to the left, resulted in 
the original 6-bit chunk. I sent these to the printer, delimited by escape 
in and escape out character sequences. I then wrote an assembly language 
printer driver that, when loaded onto the PCs where the laser printers were 
connected, that were running the terminal emulation software that connected 
the PCs and printers to the mainframe, would work normally until it saw the 
escape in sequence denoting the beginning of the graphics chunks. The driver 
then reassembled the chunks and sent them to the laser printer, until the 
escape out sequence was encountered. It worked great, but explaining how 
this worked to the MIS director caused his eyes to become a bit unfocused. 
:-)

Anyway, below is a section of the table used for character plotting, and it 
is a 3 dimensional table: character, bit-row, bit-column.

      ******************************************************************
      *                                                                *
      *          L E T T E R   B I T   M A S K   T A B L E             *
      *                                                                *
      *    THIS IS THE LETTER 'BIT MASK' TABLE FOR THE GRAPHICS        *
      *    PRIMITIVE DRAW LETTER ROUTINE.                              *
      *                                                                *
      ******************************************************************
       01  LETTER-BIT-TABLE.
           03  LBT-VALUES.
               05  FILLER      PIC  X(01)       VALUE "?".
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 01111100.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 11000110.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 11000110.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00001100.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
      *
               ...
      *
               05  FILLER      PIC  X(01)       VALUE ",".
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00000000.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00001100.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00001100.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00001100.
               05  FILLER      PIC  9(08)  COMP VALUE 00011000.
      *
           03  LBT-LETTERS         REDEFINES LBT-VALUES
                                   OCCURS 18 TIMES
                                   INDEXED BY LBT-XLET.
               05  LBT-LETTER          PIC  X(01).
               05  LBT-ROW             OCCURS 11 TIMES
                                       INDEXED BY LBT-XROW.
                   07  LBT-BIT             PIC  9(01)  COMP
                                           OCCURS 8 TIMES
                                           INDEXED BY LBT-XBIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL-THREE-level table

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-11-23T10:05:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dm2b1p$v16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com> <dm1ps9$f2e$1@reader2.panix.com> <Xf%gf.15900$s92.14365@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Xf%gf.15900$s92.14365@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> Curious, I did a brief search for times I had used a 3 dimensional table 
> (don't think I ever used more than 3), and came up with the following. I 
> think the background for this program is interesting.

It's not surprising that you didn't use more than three dimensions very 
often; in ANSI X3.23-1974, anything beyond that is an implementor-defined 
extension.   ANSI X3.23-1985 increased this to require support for 
seven-dimensional tables.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **References:** `<1132708820.713715.219230@g44g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <11o7pgul7e6ejec@news.supernews.com> <dm1ps9$f2e$1@reader2.panix.com> <Xf%gf.15900$s92.14365@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <dm2b1p$v16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jy4hf.16522$s92.10760@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> imagining that?


Nope, I was wrong. I just referred to my trusty Burroughs Medium Systems 
COBOL 68 Manual, and it only supported 3 levels of occurs/subscripting. I 
couldn't find such a limit specified in the Medium Systems COBOL 74 manual, 
perhaps that was it? I was sure that I had worked with a COBOL compiler 
that, at least in theory, supported this many subscripting levels.
```
